Professor View 01 / 27
Business English for IT -- 16 Aulas

Rafael Pelizaro

From managing projects in Portuguese to leading international IT teams, presentations, and vendor negotiations in English

B1 São Paulo, SP -- Moema IT Project Manager 60 min / 2x week / Online
Progresso Geral0%
Speed:
Rafael Pelizaro
43 anos
IT Project Manager
São Paulo, SP -- Moema
B1 (Intermediário)
Business English + IT -- Meetings, Board Presentations, Team Management
16 aulas de 60 min (2x/semana)
Online (Zoom)

Jornada de Transformação

De: Gerente de projetos de TI que lidera em português, mas trava em reuniões internacionais com vendors e stakeholders globais

Para: IT Project Manager capaz de conduzir meetings, apresentar KPIs ao board, negociar com vendors e liderar times remotos 100% em inglês

Promessa do Programa

"From managing projects in Portuguese to leading international IT teams, presentations, and vendor negotiations in English"

Forças

  • Ampla experiência técnica em TI (bancos, e-commerce, cloud)
  • Vocabulário técnico sólido em português
  • Alta motivação: transição de carreira depende do inglês
  • Experiência com times remotos e ferramentas globais
  • Perfil analítico e organizado

Pontos de Melhoria

  • Fluência oral limitada em contextos formais
  • Dificuldade para estruturar apresentações em inglês
  • Confusão entre tempos verbais (present simple vs continuous)
  • Vocabulário de negociação e board presentations limitado
  • Pouca prática de networking em inglês

Currículo -- 16 Aulas

#TemaFoco LinguísticoAtividade PrincipalHomework
1Who Is Rafael? -- Diagnostic + Professional Self-IntroductionPresent simple, verb to beConference role-playRecord 90-sec self-intro
2Running the Meeting -- Leading Status UpdatesPresent continuous, meeting vocabularyStatus meeting simulationWrite meeting agenda
3Presenting Numbers -- Data and KPIs for the BoardComparatives, numbers/percentagesKPI presentation practiceRecord 2-min data update
4Managing Vendors -- Negotiation BasicsWould for proposals and counteroffersReading a vendor negotiation + True/FalseRecord a vendor counteroffer
5The Sprint Review -- Technical Updates for Non-Tech PeopleDefining relative clauses (that / which / who)Reading a sprint update + True/FalseRecord a plain-English sprint update
6Handling Pushback -- Disagreeing ProfessionallyFirst conditional, opinion framesReading a pushback case + True/FalseRecord a disagreement recap
7The Board Presentation -- Executive CommunicationFormal connectors and registerReading a board brief + True/FalseRecord a 1-min board update
8Remote Team Management -- Async CommunicationImperative, polite requestsSlack/email writing practiceWrite async update
9Risk Assessment -- Reporting Problems and SolutionsModals (should, must, might)Risk report presentationRecord risk assessment
10The International Call -- Cross-Cultural CommunicationIndirect language, clarificationMulti-timezone call simulationReflection journal
11Project Proposal -- Pitching Ideas to LeadershipFuture forms (will, going to)Pitch presentation practiceWrite project proposal
12Quarterly Review -- Summarizing ResultsPresent perfect for resultsReading a quarterly report + True/FalseRecord a quarterly recap
13Onboarding a New Vendor -- Contract DiscussionSecond conditional, legal vocabularyContract discussion role-playReview contract terms
14Crisis Communication -- When Things Go WrongPast continuous, crisis vocabularyIncident report simulationWrite incident email
15The Job Interview -- Selling Your ExperiencePresent perfect + past simpleMock interview practiceRecord interview answers
16Full Simulation -- International IT Manager DayAll structures integrated40-min immersion simulationFinal reflection audio

Welcome, Rafael!

Onboarding

Welcome to your program! Before each lesson, complete the Pre-class activities. Here are 5 emergency phrases you can use anytime:

1 Could you repeat that, please?
2 I am not sure I understand. Could you explain?
3 Let me think about that for a moment.
4 That is a great question.
5 In my experience, I would say...
Lesson 01 -- Pre-class

Who Is Rafael Pelizaro?

Diagnostic + Professional Self-Introduction: Present simple & verb to be
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key words for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Manage -- to be in charge of and make decisions about a project or team
"I manage three IT projects for a major bank in Brazil."
Lead -- to direct and guide a team toward a goal
"Rafael leads a remote team of twelve developers."
Implement -- to put a plan, system, or idea into action
"We plan to implement the new ERP module next quarter."
Deploy -- to release software or updates to a production environment
"The IT team will deploy the update on Friday."
Report -- to give a formal account of progress or results to someone
"I need to report our progress to the board this afternoon."
Stakeholder -- a person with an interest or concern in a project's outcome
"The stakeholders are expecting a detailed project update."
Vendor -- an external company that provides products or services
"We are negotiating with a new vendor for cloud services."
Milestone -- an important stage or event in the development of a project
"We reached an important milestone last month."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Manage
Lead
Implement
Deploy
Report
Stakeholder
Vendor
Milestone

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Read the text about Rafael. Pay attention to the words in bold.

Rafael is an IT Project Manager based in São Paulo. He manages technology systems for banks and e-commerce companies. Every day, he leads meetings with his remote team and reports progress to senior executives. He implements new solutions and deploys updates regularly. His stakeholders include CFOs, CTOs, and business directors. Rafael works with several international vendors for cloud infrastructure. Last quarter, his team reached an important milestone: they deployed a new payment system for a major bank. Rafael wants to work for a 100% international company, so he studies English to present projects, lead meetings, and manage teams in English.
1. What does Rafael manage?
A He manages a restaurant in São Paulo.
B He manages technology systems for banks and e-commerce companies.
C He manages a team of marketing professionals.
2. What was the milestone his team reached?
A They hired twelve new developers.
B They moved to a new office.
C They deployed a new payment system for a major bank.
3. Why does Rafael study English?
A To present projects, lead meetings, and manage teams in English.
B To travel to Singapore for vacation.
C To read technical documentation only.

Grammar Tip: Present Simple

Grammar

Present Simple is used to describe routines, habits, facts, and permanent situations.

FormStructureExample
AffirmativeSubject + verb (+ s/es for he/she/it)He manages IT projects.
NegativeSubject + do/does + not + base verbHe does not manage the HR team.
QuestionDo/Does + subject + base verb?Does he manage IT projects?

Key rule: For he/she/it, add -s or -es to the verb. For I/you/we/they, use the base form. Time markers: every day, always, usually, never, on Fridays.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"Rafael technology systems for banks."
"He a remote team of twelve developers."
"We plan to the new ERP module next quarter."
"The IT team will the update on Friday."
"I need to our progress to the board."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Listen to the audio, then put the sentences in the correct order.

? "I lead a remote team of twelve developers."
? "I am Rafael Pelizaro, an IT Project Manager."
? "I report our progress to stakeholders every week."
? "I manage technology systems for banks and e-commerce."
? "We implement and deploy solutions for major clients."

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

Speaking

Listen to the model, then record yourself. Compare your pronunciation word by word.

I am an IT Project Manager. I manage technology systems for banks and e-commerce companies.
Every week, I report our progress to stakeholders and manage meetings with vendors.
We implemented and deployed a new payment platform for a major bank last quarter.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. A colleague at a conference asks: "What do you do?" What is the best professional response?
A "I do computer things for companies."
B "I am an IT Project Manager. I manage technology systems for banks and e-commerce companies."
C "I work with technology. It is complicated."
2. A vendor asks: "How do you manage communication with a remote team?" What is the most complete answer?
A "We use email."
B "It is difficult but we manage."
C "I lead daily meetings with my team, and I report our progress to stakeholders every week."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are at an international tech conference in Singapore. Someone asks: "Tell me about yourself and your work." Record a 60-second professional self-introduction. Try to use at least 5 vocabulary words from this lesson (manage, lead, implement, deploy, report, stakeholder, vendor, milestone).

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1 I am an IT Project Manager based in São Paulo.
2 I manage technology systems for banks and e-commerce.
3 I lead a remote team of twelve developers.
4 We deployed a new payment platform last quarter.
5 I would love to discuss this further.
Lesson 02 -- Pre-class

Running the Meeting -- Leading Status Updates

Present Continuous + Meeting vocabulary for IT professionals
0%
▼
Vocabulary

Stage 1.1: Key Vocabulary

Click on each card to listen. Learn 8 essential meeting words.

Agenda.A list of topics to be discussed during a meeting
"Let me share the agenda for today's meeting."
Action item.A specific task assigned to someone during or after a meeting
"We have three action items from last week's meeting."
Follow up.To check on or continue something that was discussed
"I will follow up with the vendor about the delivery date."
Status update.A report on the current progress of a project or task
"Can you give us a status update on the deployment?"
Deadline.The final date by which something must be completed
"The deadline for the new module is next Friday."
Prioritize.To decide the order of importance of tasks
"We need to prioritize the critical bugs before the release."
Reschedule.To change the planned time or date of a meeting
"Can we reschedule the meeting to Thursday afternoon?"
Minutes.The official written record of what was discussed in a meeting
"I will send the meeting minutes by end of day."
Practice

Stage 1.2: Match the Word to Its Definition

Agenda
Action item
Follow up
Status update
Deadline
Prioritize
Reschedule
Minutes
Grammar

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

It is Monday morning and Rafael is leading his weekly status meeting via Zoom. His team is working on the new payment module. Lisa, the QA engineer, is testing the system right now and her team is finding some critical bugs. Rafael is prioritizing the tasks and is following up with the vendor about the cloud service agreement. They are approaching the end-of-quarter deadline, so everyone is working hard to meet it. After the meeting, Rafael will send the minutes and the action items to the whole team.

What is Rafael doing right now?
A He is writing the QA report.
B He is leading his weekly status meeting.
C He is deploying the payment module.
What is Lisa's team finding?
A New vendors for cloud services.
B Missing meeting minutes.
C Critical bugs in the payment module.
Why is everyone working hard?
A They are approaching the end-of-quarter deadline.
B The vendor is canceling the contract.
C Rafael is rescheduling all meetings.
Grammar

Grammar Tip: Present Continuous

Form: Subject + am / is / are + verb-ing

Use it for:

  • Actions happening right now: "We are discussing the budget."
  • Temporary situations: "I am working on the migration this week."
  • Future arrangements: "We are meeting the vendor on Thursday."
Subject Form Example
I am + verb-ing I am preparing the agenda.
He / She / It is + verb-ing He is leading the meeting.
We / You / They are + verb-ing We are discussing the deadlines.
Common mistake: "He leading the meeting" ✗ → "He is leading the meeting." ✓
Practice

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

"Rafael ___ a status meeting with his team right now."
"Lisa ___ the payment module right now."
"The team ___ on fixes for the critical bugs."
"We ___ with the vendor this week."
"He ___ the tasks before the deadline."
Order

Stage 2: Put the Meeting in Order

Listen to the meeting sequence, then arrange the steps in the correct order.

? "Lisa gives a status update on the QA testing."
? "Rafael shares the agenda for the meeting."
? "Rafael takes the minutes and sends them to the team."
? "Rafael asks the team for updates."
? "They discuss rescheduling the deployment deadline."
Speaking

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

I am preparing the agenda for our status meeting and following up on last week's action items.
We are meeting at 10 AM on Monday via Zoom.
The QA team is testing the payment module right now.
Quiz

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Your team member asks: "What are we discussing today?" You reply:
A "I discussed the agenda yesterday."
B "We are discussing the deployment status, QA report, and deadlines."
C "We will to discuss three topics."
A stakeholder asks about the QA progress. You respond:
A "Lisa tested the module last week."
B "Lisa test the module right now."
C "Lisa is testing the payment module right now and finding some bugs."
Reflection

Stage 5: Think About It

Think about your next team meeting. What topics are on your agenda? What tasks are you following up on? Record yourself giving a 30-second status update using Present Continuous.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1 Let me share the agenda for today's meeting.
2 Can you give us a status update on your tasks?
3 We need to follow up on the action items from last week.
4 I am prioritizing the critical bugs before the deadline.
5 Let me take the minutes and send them after the meeting.
Lesson 03 -- Pre-class

Presenting Numbers -- Data and KPIs for the Board

Comparatives + Numbers/Percentages
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Key Vocabulary

Vocabulary

Eight essential words for presenting data and KPIs to the board.

KPI.A measurable value that shows how well a company is achieving its goals
"Our main KPI this quarter is customer acquisition cost."
Revenue.The total money a company earns from its business activities
"Revenue increased by 12 percent compared to last quarter."
Growth.An increase in size, value, or amount over time
"We are seeing strong growth in the cloud services division."
Decline.A decrease or reduction in numbers, quality, or importance
"There was a slight decline in hardware sales this month."
Quarter.A three-month period used to divide the business year (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4)
"Q2 results were higher than Q1 across all departments."
Budget.The planned amount of money available for spending
"We need to stay within the budget for the next quarter."
Forecast.A prediction of future business results based on current data
"The forecast shows a 15 percent increase in revenue for Q3."
ROI.Return on Investment -- the profit earned relative to the money spent
"The ROI on our cloud migration project exceeded expectations."

Stage 1.2: Match the Definitions

Practice
KPI
Revenue
Growth
Decline
Quarter
Budget
Forecast
ROI

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Rafael is presenting Q2 results to the board. Read the text and pay attention to the words in bold.

"Good afternoon, everyone. Our Q2 revenue was 2.3 million dollars, which is higher than Q1 by 12 percent. Cloud services generated 1.4 million, making the cloud division more profitable than on-premise. Our customer acquisition cost is lower than our target, which is excellent. The ROI on the migration project was 3.2 times, which is better than the industry average. Looking at the forecast, we expect growth of 15 percent in Q3, but hardware may see a slight decline. Our budget utilization was 95 percent, and spending was lower than the approved amount."
1. How does Q2 revenue compare to Q1?
A It is the same as Q1.
B It is 12 percent higher than Q1.
C It is lower than Q1.
2. Which division is more profitable?
A On-premise is more profitable than cloud.
B Cloud is more profitable than on-premise.
C They are equally profitable.
3. What does the forecast show for Q3?
A A decline of 15 percent.
B No change from Q2.
C Growth of 15 percent.

Grammar Tip: Comparatives

Grammar

We use comparatives to compare two things. This is essential when presenting data and KPIs.

TypeRuleExample
Short adjectivesadjective + -er thanRevenue is higher than last quarter.
Long adjectivesmore + adjective + thanCloud is more profitable than on-premise.
Irregulargood → better thanQ2 results are better than Q1.
Irregularbad → worse thanHardware sales are worse than expected.

Common patterns for data: "X is [number] percent higher/lower than Y." | "Division A is more/less profitable than Division B." | "The ROI is better/worse than the forecast."

Stage 1.5: Complete the Sentences

Practice
"Revenue in Q2 was than in Q1."
"The cloud division is than hardware."
"Our costs this quarter are than last quarter."
"Growth in LATAM is than in Europe."
"The new forecast is than the original estimate."

Stage 2: Vocabulary Practice

Practice
The company's _____ for Q3 predicts 15% growth.
A budget
B forecast
C decline
Hardware sales showed a 5% _____ this month.
A growth
B decline
C revenue
The _____ on the cloud migration was 3.2 times our investment.
A ROI
B KPI
C quarter

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

Speaking
Our Q2 revenue was 2.3 million dollars, which is 12 percent higher than Q1.
Cloud services are more profitable than on-premise solutions for our company.
The forecast for Q3 shows continued growth and lower operational costs.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
The CFO asks: "How does Q2 compare to Q1?" What do you say?
A "Q2 is good and Q1 was also good."
B "Revenue in Q2 was 12 percent higher than Q1."
C "I think Q2 was probably better."
A board member asks: "Is the cloud migration worth it?" What do you say?
A "Yes, the ROI was 3.2 times, which is better than the industry average."
B "I am not sure about the numbers."
C "Cloud is nice."
Someone asks: "What is the forecast for next quarter?" What do you say?
A "Maybe it will be okay."
B "I have not checked the numbers yet."
C "The forecast shows 15 percent growth, which is higher than our original estimate."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are presenting your team's Q2 results to the board. Record a 60-second update using comparatives.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1Revenue increased by 12 percent compared to last quarter.
2Our costs are lower than the approved budget.
3The ROI exceeded our forecast by a significant margin.
4Cloud services are more profitable than on-premise solutions.
5The forecast for Q3 shows a 15 percent increase in revenue.
Aula 4 -- Pre-class

Managing Vendors -- Negotiation Basics

Reading a real vendor negotiation and making polite proposals and counteroffers with would (Would you consider...?, We would accept...). New vocabulary: negotiate, supplier, quote, proposal, terms, discount, counteroffer, agreement, flexible, commitment.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Negotiate -- to talk with someone to reach a deal both sides accept
"We negotiate the software price every year." (Negociar)
Supplier -- a company that provides goods or services to your business
"The supplier sent us a quote for the licenses." (Fornecedor)
Quote -- a price a supplier offers for a product or service
"The first quote was over our budget." (Cotação)
Proposal -- a formal offer or plan you present to the other side
"Their proposal looked good, but the price was high." (Proposta)
Terms -- the conditions of a deal: price, length and payment
"We agreed on the terms of the contract." (Termos / condições)
Discount -- a reduction in the normal price
"She offered a ten percent discount." (Desconto)
Counteroffer -- a new offer you make in reply, with different terms
"I made a counteroffer with support included." (Contraproposta)
Agreement -- when both sides accept the same terms
"We reached an agreement that works for both." (Acordo)
Flexible -- willing to change or adapt to find a deal
"The supplier was flexible on the price." (Flexível)
Commitment -- a firm promise to do something, like sign for a period
"We offered a two-year commitment." (Compromisso)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Negotiate
Supplier
Quote
Proposal
Terms
Discount
Counteroffer
Agreement

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Rafael has to renew the team's software, and the first quote from the supplier is over budget. He reads the proposal and prepares his terms before the call. He stays polite. "Would you consider a discount," he asks, "for a two-year commitment?" The supplier is flexible. She offers ten percent off, but she would need faster payment. Rafael makes a counteroffer: "That would work. We would accept the longer term, but only with support included." After a few minutes, they reach an agreement that works for both sides. Rafael uses would to keep every offer polite and soft.

1. What does Rafael ask the supplier for?
A A discount in exchange for a longer commitment.
B A full refund of last year's contract.
C A completely new supplier.
2. Why does Rafael use would so much?
A To talk about a finished moment in the past.
B To keep his offers and requests polite and soft.
C To give the supplier a direct order.
3. What does Rafael require in his counteroffer?
A A higher price for faster delivery.
B Nothing in return for the discount.
C Support included at no extra cost.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Would for Proposals

GRAMMAR

Usamos would para fazer ofertas e pedidos mais educados e suaves numa negociação. / We use would to make offers and requests softer and more polite in a negotiation.

Form / FormaStructureExample / Exemplo
Affirmative / Afirmativowould + base verbWe would accept these terms.
Question / PerguntaWould + subject + base verb?Would you consider a discount?
Negative / Negativowould not (wouldn't) + base verbWe would not accept that price.
Contraction / ContraçãoI'd / we'd / that'd + base verbThat'd work for both sides.

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de would, use o verbo na base, sem "to" (would suggest, não would to suggest) e nunca no passado (would accept, não would accepted). Use would para soar educado: "Would you consider...?" é muito mais suave que "Give me a discount."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Would you a discount for a longer deal?"
"We would the terms with full support."
"The first is over our budget."
"I made a with support included."
"We reached an that works for both sides."

Stage 2: Put the Negotiation in Order

Order

Put the steps of a polite negotiation in the correct order.

?Then, offer something in return, like a longer commitment.
?First, thank the supplier and say the price is over budget.
?Finally, confirm the agreement out loud.
?Next, ask for a discount politely with would.
?After that, respond to the counteroffer with your own terms.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a calm, polite tone.

Would you consider a discount for a longer deal?
Você consideraria um desconto para um contrato mais longo?
We would accept the terms with support included.
Nós aceitaríamos os termos com suporte incluído.
The first quote is a little over our budget this year.
A primeira cotação está um pouco acima do nosso orçamento este ano.
I would suggest a two-year commitment.
Eu sugeriria um compromisso de dois anos.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each negotiation situation.

The supplier's price is too high. The most polite way to ask for less is:
A "Give me a lower price."
B "Would you consider a discount for a longer deal?"
C "I would to ask a discount."
You want to accept a trade but add one condition. You say:
A "We would accept, but only with support included."
B "We would accepted the offer."
C "We accept nothing today."
To suggest a two-year deal politely, you say:
A "I would suggesting two years."
B "I would suggest a two-year commitment."
C "I will force a two-year deal."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a supplier's quote is over your budget. Record a short negotiation out loud: thank them, say the price is too high, ask for a discount with would, offer a longer commitment in return, and make a counteroffer that asks for support included. Use would to stay polite (Would you consider...?, We would accept...).

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1Thank you for the proposal, but the price is a little high.Obrigado pela proposta, mas o preço está um pouco alto.
2Would you consider a discount for a longer commitment?Você consideraria um desconto para um compromisso mais longo?
3We would accept faster payment if support is included.Nós aceitaríamos pagamento mais rápido se o suporte estiver incluído.
4That would work. Let me make a small counteroffer.Isso funcionaria. Deixa eu fazer uma pequena contraproposta.
5Great, I think we have an agreement.Ótimo, acho que temos um acordo.
Aula 5 -- Pre-class

The Sprint Review -- Technical Updates for Non-Tech People

Reading a real sprint update and explaining technical work in plain terms with defining relative clauses (a button that lets you reset, a person who cares, the list which holds the tasks). New vocabulary: sprint, demo, backlog, outsider, rephrase, simplify, analogy, summary, feature, bug.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Sprint -- a short period of work, usually two weeks, to build one part of a product
"This sprint we built a new password button." (Ciclo de trabalho)
Demo -- a short, live showing of the work in action
"Then I show the demo of what already works." (Demonstração)
Backlog -- the list of tasks and ideas still waiting to be built
"A backlog is the list of tasks that are waiting." (Lista de pendências)
Outsider -- someone who is not part of the technical team
"I explain the work so an outsider can follow it." (Leigo)
Rephrase -- to say something again in simpler, clearer words
"If a word is too technical, I rephrase it." (Reformular)
Simplify -- to make something easier to understand
"My job is to simplify the update for the room." (Simplificar)
Analogy -- a comparison that explains a new idea with a familiar one
"I like a simple analogy: our app is like a house." (Analogia)
Summary -- a short description of the whole thing, the main points only
"A good summary gives the big picture first." (Resumo)
Feature -- a specific function a user can use in a product
"The photo feature is not ready yet." (Funcionalidade)
Bug -- a fault that makes part of the software work badly
"We found a bug that slowed the login screen." (Falha / erro)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Sprint
Demo
Backlog
Outsider
Rephrase
Analogy
Summary
Bug

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before the sprint review, Rafael writes a short note for the outsiders in the room. He knows most of them are not engineers, so he wants to simplify every line. "A backlog is the list that holds the tasks we have not built yet," he writes, and he rephrases the jargon. For the new feature, he says: "It is a button which lets a customer reset a password alone." He adds a small analogy — the app is like a house, and this sprint they built the front door. He is honest about the bug too: "We found a problem that slowed the login, so one part needs one more week." He ends with a one-line summary and an invitation to the live demo, because anyone who can see the work will trust it.

1. How does Rafael define a backlog for the stakeholders?
A As a finished feature ready to launch.
B As the list that holds the tasks they have not built yet.
C As a bug in the login screen.
2. Why does Rafael add the house analogy?
A To make a technical idea easy to picture.
B To show the team built a real house.
C To hide the bug from the stakeholders.
3. Why does he end with a live demo?
A Because slides are always better than a demo.
B Because the feature is fully finished.
C Because anyone who can see the work will trust it.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Defining Relative Clauses

GRAMMAR

Usamos that / which / who para DEFINIR de qual coisa ou pessoa falamos — ideal para explicar jargão em palavras simples. / We use that / which / who to DEFINE which thing or person we mean — perfect for explaining jargon in plain words.

Word / PalavraUse / UsoExample / Exemplo
thatcoisas ou pessoas (neutro, muito comum)a button that lets you reset
whichapenas coisas / only thingsthe list which holds the tasks
whoapenas pessoas / only peoplea person who cares
Structure / Estruturanoun + that / which / who + verba bug that slowed the login

Key point / Ponto-chave: nunca use what no lugar de that / which ("a button what lets you reset" está errado). E não repita o sujeito depois do pronome: "a person who he cares" está errado — o certo é "a person who cares".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"A backlog is the list holds the waiting tasks."
"A stakeholder is a person cares about the result."
"My job is to the update for the room."
"I will show the of what already works."
"We found a bug slowed the login screen."

Stage 2: Put the Update in Order

Order

Put the steps of a clear, non-technical sprint update in the correct order.

?After that, use a simple analogy if the idea is complex.
?First, give a one-line overview with no jargon.
?Finally, show the live demo of what already works.
?Next, define any technical term with a relative clause.
?Then, be honest about one bug, in plain words.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a calm, clear tone.

It is a button that lets a customer reset a password.
É um botão que permite ao cliente redefinir a senha.
A backlog is the list that holds the waiting tasks.
Um backlog é a lista que contém as tarefas em espera.
Let me give you a quick summary, in plain terms.
Deixa eu te dar uma visão geral rápida, em palavras simples.
We found a bug that slowed the login, so it needs one more week.
Achamos uma falha que deixou o login lento, então precisa de mais uma semana.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each sprint review situation.

A non-technical director asks what your team built. The clearest answer is:
A "We built a button that lets customers reset their password."
B "We refactored the auth endpoint and cleared the backlog."
C "We did a lot of technical things this sprint."
A stakeholder does not know the word "backlog". You say:
A "A backlog is the list what holds the tasks."
B "A backlog is the list that holds the tasks still waiting."
C "A backlog is a person who waits for tasks."
A feature is not ready because of a bug. The most honest, simple update is:
A "Everything is perfect and fully done."
B "The photo part has a bug, so it needs one more week."
C "The mutex on the worker thread deadlocked again."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are presenting your last sprint to a non-technical outsider. Record a short update out loud: give a one-line overview, say what you built and define one term with a relative clause (a button that..., a list which..., a person who...), admit one honest bug, and invite them to the demo.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1Let me give you a quick summary, in plain terms.Deixa eu te dar uma visão geral rápida, em palavras simples.
2It is a button that lets a customer reset a password.É um botão que permite ao cliente redefinir a senha.
3A backlog is the list that holds the waiting tasks.Um backlog é a lista que contém as tarefas em espera.
4We found a bug, so it needs one more week.Achamos uma falha, então precisa de mais uma semana.
5Let me show you the demo of what already works.Deixa eu te mostrar a demo do que já funciona.
Aula 6 -- Pre-class

Handling Pushback -- Disagreeing Professionally

Reading a real pushback case and disagreeing professionally with the first conditional (If we add it now, we will miss the deadline) and polite opinion frames. New vocabulary: pushback, concern, objection, constraint, trade-off, compromise, assumption, align, justify, reasonable.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Pushback -- resistance or disagreement with a plan
"The team gave some pushback on the new deadline." (Resistência)
Concern -- a worry about a possible problem
"I have a concern about the deadline on Friday."
Objection -- a clear reason against a plan or idea
"My objection is the timing, not the feature itself." (Objeção)
Constraint -- a limit that restricts what you can do
"Time is our main constraint this week."
Trade-off -- accept one loss to gain something else
"There is a trade-off between speed and quality." (Compensação / troca)
Compromise -- an agreement where each side gives up a little
"As a compromise, we moved it to the next sprint."
Assumption -- something you accept as true without checking it
"My assumption is that the client wants it next month." (Suposição)
Align -- to bring people to the same plan or agreement
"I want us to align before we call the client."
Justify -- to give good reasons for a decision or position
"Let me justify my objection with the numbers." (Justificar)
Reasonable -- fair and sensible; not extreme
"That is a reasonable compromise for both sides."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Pushback
Concern
Objection
Constraint
Trade-off
Compromise
Assumption
Align
Justify
Reasonable

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The product owner gives Rafael some pushback: she wants a new login feature in the current sprint. Rafael does not say no. He raises a concern about the deadline. "If we add this feature now, we will miss the milestone on Friday," he writes. His objection is the timing, not the feature. He explains the constraint on time and the trade-off with quality. Then he offers a compromise: "If we move it to the next sprint, we will protect the deadline." He checks his assumption about the client, justifies the plan with clear reasons, and asks to align. In the end, both sides reach a reasonable agreement.

1. What does Rafael do instead of saying no?
A He ignores the product owner.
B He raises a concern and explains the consequence.
C He adds the feature without testing.
2. What is Rafael's objection?
A The timing of the feature, not the feature itself.
B The feature is a bad idea.
C The client is wrong.
3. What compromise does Rafael offer?
A To cancel the feature completely.
B To skip the testing phase.
C To move the feature to the next sprint to protect the deadline.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The First Conditional

GRAMMAR

Usamos a primeira condicional para falar de uma condição real e seu resultado provável. / We use the first conditional for a real condition and its likely result.

Form / FormaStructureExample / Exemplo
Certain result / Resultado certoIf + present simple, ... will + base verbIf we add it now, we will miss the deadline.
Possible result / Resultado possívelIf + present simple, ... may + base verbIf the vendor is late, we may need a backup plan.
Negative / Negativo... will not + base verbIf we cut the scope, the client will not be happy.
Opinion frame / Frase de opiniãoI see your point, but... / I agreeI see your point, but I have a concern.

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de if, use o present simple, NUNCA "will" ("If we add", não "If we will add"). O will ou may vai no resultado. E lembre: agree já é verbo — diga "I agree", nunca "I am agree".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I see your point, but I have a about the deadline."
"If we it now, we will miss the deadline."
"As a , could we move it to the next sprint?"
"There is a between speed and quality."
"I want us to before we call the client."

Stage 2: Put the Disagreement in Order

Order

Put the steps of a professional disagreement in the correct order.

?Then, explain the consequence with a first conditional.
?First, see the other person's point: "I see your point, but..."
?Finally, confirm the next step and align positively.
?Next, state your concern and your objection clearly.
?After that, offer a compromise instead of just saying no.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a calm, professional tone.

I see your point, but I have a concern about the deadline.
Entendo o seu ponto, mas tenho uma preocupação com o prazo.
If we add this feature now, we will miss the milestone.
Se adicionarmos essa funcionalidade agora, vamos perder o marco.
As a compromise, could we move it to the next sprint?
Como meio-termo, poderíamos movê-la para o próximo sprint?
I am glad we align. I will update the plan today.
Que bom que estamos alinhados. Vou atualizar o plano hoje.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the most professional response for each situation.

A stakeholder wants to add work to your sprint. The best reply is:
A "I see your point, but I have a concern about the deadline."
B "No. That is a bad idea."
C "Just do it my way."
You want to explain the consequence of a late decision. You say:
A "If we will add it, we will miss the deadline."
B "If we add it now, we will miss the deadline."
C "If we add it now, we miss the deadline tomorrow."
You want to show agreement politely. The correct sentence is:
A "I am agree with your concern."
B "I agree with your concern, and I have a compromise."
C "I am agreeing your concern."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a stakeholder pushes back on your plan and wants to add work this week. Record a short, professional disagreement out loud: see their point, raise one concern, explain the consequence with a first conditional (if we... we will...), offer a compromise, and align on the next step.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1I see your point, but I have a concern about the deadline.Entendo o seu ponto, mas tenho uma preocupação com o prazo.
2If we add it now, we will miss the milestone.Se adicionarmos agora, vamos perder o marco.
3My objection is the timing, not the feature itself.Minha objeção é o momento, não a funcionalidade em si.
4As a compromise, could we move it to the next sprint?Como meio-termo, poderíamos movê-la para o próximo sprint?
5I am glad we align. If you agree, I will update the plan today.Que bom que estamos alinhados. Se concordar, atualizo o plano hoje.
Aula 7 -- Pre-class

The Board Presentation -- Executive Communication

Reading a real board brief and linking ideas with formal connectors (In addition, However, As a result, To summarize). New vocabulary: board, handout, slide, audience, overview, highlight, summarize, key point, recommendation, confident.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Board -- the group of senior directors who make the top decisions
"The board has ten minutes for your update." (Diretoria / conselho)
Handout -- a printed page you give the audience to follow along
"Each director gets a one-page handout." (Folheto / material de apoio)
Slide -- one single screen or page of a presentation
"They read every slide carefully." (Slide)
Audience -- the group of people you are speaking or presenting to
"The board is a tough audience." (Plateia / audiência)
Overview -- a short, general summary you give at the beginning
"Let me give you a brief overview." (Visão geral)
Highlight -- an important, positive point you want people to notice
"Let me share two highlights." (Destaque)
Summarize -- to give only the main points in a short, clear way
"To summarize, the platform is faster and cheaper." (Resumir)
Key point -- the single most important idea you want people to remember
"They want the key point first." (Ponto-chave)
Recommendation -- what you advise the group to do next
"My recommendation is to approve the go-live." (Recomendação)
Confident -- calm and sure of yourself, clear and in control
"Leave the directors confident in the project." (Confiante)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Board
Handout
Audience
Overview
Highlight
Summarize
Recommendation
Confident

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Rafael is preparing the board presentation for the Phoenix platform. He knows the directors are a busy audience, so he keeps the agenda short and puts the key point on the first slide. He opens with a one-paragraph overview. Then he shares two highlights. "The platform is faster. In addition, it is cheaper," he writes. He is also honest. "The pilot works well. However, the security review took longer. As a result, go-live moves to April." Finally, he closes the agenda. "To summarize, the delay is under control, and my recommendation is to approve the April go-live." He uses connectors to link every idea, because a clear, confident summary is what an executive audience remembers.

1. Where does Rafael put the key point?
A On the first slide, in a short overview.
B On the very last slide, after all the detail.
C He does not mention a key point.
2. Which connector does he use to introduce the security issue?
A "In addition" -- to add a good point.
B "However" -- to show a contrast.
C "To summarize" -- to close.
3. What is Rafael's recommendation?
A To cancel the project.
B To remove every slide from the deck.
C To approve the April go-live.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Formal Connectors

GRAMMAR

Usamos conectores para ligar ideias numa apresentação formal. Eles começam uma nova frase e levam vírgula depois. / We use connectors to link ideas in a formal presentation. They start a new sentence and take a comma after them.

Job / FunçãoConnectorsExample / Exemplo
Add / AdicionarIn addition, Also, MoreoverIt is faster. In addition, it is cheaper.
Contrast / ContrasteHowever, On the other handIt works. However, the review was slow.
Result / ResultadoAs a result, Therefore, SoIt was slow. As a result, go-live moves.
Sum up / ResumirTo summarize, In short, OverallTo summarize, the delay is under control.

Key point / Ponto-chave: o conector começa uma nova frase e leva vírgula logo depois: "..., it is cheaper." Cuidado: However não é although -- não use "However the budget is tight, ..."; use "The budget is tight. However, ...". E não cole duas frases só com vírgula antes de therefore.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct connector. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The platform is faster. , it is cheaper."
"The pilot works well. , the review took longer."
"The review took longer. , go-live moves to April."
", the delay is under control."
"My is to approve the April go-live."

Stage 2: Put the Brief in Order

Order

Put the steps of a clear board presentation in the correct order.

?Then, be honest about one issue and its result.
?First, open with the key point in a one-paragraph overview.
?Finally, close with one clear recommendation.
?Next, share two highlights linked with "In addition".
?After that, summarize the whole project in one line.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a calm, confident tone.

Let me give you a brief overview of the project.
Deixe-me dar uma visão geral rápida do projeto.
The platform is faster. In addition, it is cheaper.
A plataforma é mais rápida. Além disso, é mais barata.
The review took longer. As a result, go-live moves to April.
A revisão demorou mais. Como resultado, o go-live vai para abril.
To summarize, my recommendation is to approve the go-live.
Resumindo, minha recomendação é aprovar o go-live.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each board situation.

The board has ten minutes and asks you to begin. The best opening is:
A "Let me give you a brief overview. Then I will share two highlights."
B "Let me explain every slide in full detail first."
C "I am not sure where to start, sorry."
You want to add a second positive result. You say:
A "However, the cost is lower."
B "In addition, the cost is lower."
C "To summarize the cost is lower."
A delay pushes go-live by a month. You report it as:
A "The review took longer, therefore go-live moves to April."
B "The review took longer. As a result, go-live moves to April."
C "However the review took longer, go-live moves to April."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you have ten minutes with your board. Record a short brief out loud: give a one-line overview, share two highlights (link them with "In addition"), admit one issue (use "However" and "As a result"), then close with one recommendation (start with "To summarize"). Keep a calm, confident tone.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1Let me give you a brief overview of the project.Deixe-me dar uma visão geral rápida do projeto.
2The platform is faster. In addition, it is cheaper.A plataforma é mais rápida. Além disso, é mais barata.
3The review took longer. As a result, go-live moves to April.A revisão demorou mais. Como resultado, o go-live vai para abril.
4To summarize, the delay is under control.Resumindo, o atraso está sob controle.
5My recommendation is to approve the April go-live.Minha recomendação é aprovar o go-live de abril.
Aula 08 -- Pre-class

Remote Team Management -- Async Communication

Leading a distributed team across time zones with short, clear written messages: clear imperatives for steps and polite requests (please, could you, would you mind + -ing). New vocabulary: asynchronous, handoff, thread, time zone, availability, heads-up, loop in, blocker.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Asynchronous -- communication that does not need an immediate reply
"Our team uses asynchronous updates across time zones." (Assíncrono)
Handoff -- passing a task to a teammate, often in another time zone
"I left a clear handoff note for the night team." (Repasse)
Thread -- a series of messages about one topic
"Please keep all the bug reports in one thread." (Tópico / conversa)
Time zone -- the local clock time of a region
"Marta is six hours ahead in her time zone." (Fuso horário)
Availability -- the hours when you are free to work or reply
"Could you share your availability for tomorrow?" (Disponibilidade)
Heads-up -- an early, informal warning or notice
"Thanks for the heads-up about the delay." (Aviso prévio)
Loop in -- to add someone to a message so they stay informed
"Please loop in Marta on the final decision." (Incluir / dar ciência)
Blocker -- something that stops your work until it is solved
"The missing access was my main blocker today." (Impedimento)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Asynchronous
Handoff
Thread
Time zone
Availability
Heads-up
Loop in
Blocker

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Rafael manages a team in different time zones, so he writes asynchronous updates that people read later. At the end of his day, he posts a short note. First, he says what is done. Then he writes a clear handoff for the night team: "Please deploy the fix when CI is green." He keeps all the reports in one thread. He is honest about his blocker: access to the test server was missing, so he gives a heads-up that the admin is fixing it. Finally, he makes two polite requests: "Could you share your availability for tomorrow?" and "Would you mind looping in Marta?" He uses clear imperatives for steps and polite forms for people, never a demanding tone.

1. Why does Rafael write asynchronous updates?
A Because his team is in different time zones and reads later.
B Because he wants an instant reply at any hour.
C Because he prefers long phone calls.
2. How does he write the handoff step for the night team?
A "Deploy it now or there will be trouble."
B "Please deploy the fix when CI is green."
C "Maybe someone can do something later."
3. How does he ask about availability?
A "Tell me your hours right now."
B "I need your schedule immediately."
C "Could you share your availability for tomorrow?"

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Imperatives and Polite Requests

GRAMMAR

Usamos o imperativo para passos claros e formas educadas para pedir algo a uma pessoa. / We use the imperative for clear steps and polite forms to ask a person for help.

Level / NívelStructureExample / Exemplo
Direct step / Passo diretoVerb + objectSend the file.
Softened / SuavizadoPlease + verbPlease send the file when you can.
Polite request / Pedido educadoCould you + verb?Could you send the file?
Very polite / Muito educadoWould you mind + verb-ing?Would you mind sending the file?

Key point / Ponto-chave: use o imperativo para passos e checklists; para pedir a uma pessoa, suavize com please, could you ou would you mind. Depois de would you mind, use a forma -ing. Evite tom exigente como now ou immediately.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I left a clear for the night team."
" you share your availability for tomorrow?"
"Would you mind in Marta on this?"
"My only was missing server access."
"Please keep all the reports in one ."
"Thanks for the about the delay."

Stage 2: Put the Update in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a clear async update in the correct order.

?Then name any blocker and give the team a heads-up.
?First, say clearly what is done today.
?Finally, make a polite request and share your availability.
?Next, write a clear handoff step for the next team.
?After that, keep all the details in one thread.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a calm, polite tone.

Here is my async update for the team.
Aqui está minha atualização assíncrona para o time.
Please deploy the fix when CI is green.
Por favor, faça o deploy da correção quando a CI estiver verde.
Could you share your availability for tomorrow?
Você poderia compartilhar sua disponibilidade para amanhã?
Would you mind looping in Marta on this?
Você se importaria de incluir a Marta nisso?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each async situation.

You ask a teammate in another time zone for a file. The most polite message is:
A "Could you send the file when you have a moment?"
B "Send the file now. I need it immediately."
C "Why is the file not here yet?"
You want a clear step for the night team to follow. You write:
A "Maybe deploy it, or not, whenever."
B "Please deploy the fix when CI is green."
C "Would you mind to deploy the fix?"
You found a blocker that affects the release. The best async message is:
A "Heads-up: a blocker may delay the release. Details in the thread."
B "Everything is fine, do not worry about anything."
C "You broke the release again."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine it is the end of your workday and your team is in other time zones. Record a short async update out loud: say what is done, give one clear handoff step, name one blocker with a heads-up, and make two polite requests (could you / would you mind), ending with your availability.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1Here is my async update for the team.Aqui está minha atualização assíncrona para o time.
2Please deploy the fix when CI is green.Por favor, faça o deploy da correção quando a CI estiver verde.
3Could you share your availability for tomorrow?Você poderia compartilhar sua disponibilidade para amanhã?
4Would you mind looping in Marta on this?Você se importaria de incluir a Marta nisso?
5Heads-up: my only blocker was missing server access.Aviso: meu único impedimento foi a falta de acesso ao servidor.
Aula 09 -- Pre-class

Risk Assessment -- Reporting Problems and Solutions

Reporting a project risk clearly and proposing solutions: modals for possibility, recommendation, and necessity (might, should, must). New vocabulary: risk, likelihood, impact, root cause, mitigation, contingency plan, escalate, rollback.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Risk -- a possible future problem that may affect the project
"The main risk is a database overload at peak hours." (Risco)
Likelihood -- how probable it is that a problem will happen
"The likelihood of the bug is medium, not high." (Probabilidade)
Impact -- how serious the effect of a problem would be
"The impact is high, because it might cause downtime." (Impacto)
Root cause -- the deep, real reason behind a problem
"The root cause is an old query that is too slow." (Causa raiz)
Mitigation -- an action that reduces a risk or its impact
"Optimizing the query is our main mitigation." (Mitigação)
Contingency plan -- a backup plan you use if the main plan fails
"We must prepare a contingency plan for launch day." (Plano de contingência)
Escalate -- to raise an issue to a manager or higher team
"I will escalate the issue to the infrastructure team." (Escalar)
Rollback -- to return a system to a previous, safe version
"If the system slows down, we will do a rollback." (Reversão)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Risk
Likelihood
Impact
Root cause
Mitigation
Contingency plan
Escalate
Rollback

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Rafael writes a short risk assessment for the payment release. He names the main risk: a database overload at peak hours. The likelihood is medium, but the impact is high, because it might cause downtime for all users. He explains the root cause: an old query that is too slow. Then he proposes a plan. "We should optimize the query this week," he writes, "and that is our main mitigation." He adds a contingency plan: "We must prepare a backup. If the system slows down, we will do a rollback." Finally, he is clear about the next step: "If the rollback is not enough, I must escalate the issue to the infrastructure team." He uses might for what is possible, should for a recommendation, and must for what is necessary.

1. Why is the impact of this risk high?
A Because it might cause downtime for all users.
B Because the query is fast and modern.
C Because the likelihood is zero.
2. What does Rafael recommend as the main mitigation?
A Ignoring the slow query until next year.
B Optimizing the query this week.
C Adding more users at peak hours.
3. What will the team do if the system slows down?
A Nothing, they will just wait.
B Restart every computer in the office.
C Do a rollback to the previous version.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Modals: might, should, must

GRAMMAR

Usamos modais para mostrar a força de uma ideia ao reportar um risco: possibilidade, recomendação ou necessidade. / We use modals to show the strength of an idea when we report a risk: possibility, recommendation, or necessity.

ModalMeaning / SentidoExample / Exemplo
mightPossibility / PossibilidadeThe release might slip.
shouldRecommendation / RecomendaçãoWe should optimize the query.
mustStrong necessity / Necessidade forteWe must prepare a contingency plan.

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de might, should e must, use o verbo na forma base (sem to): "We must fix it" (não "must to fix"). Must é mais forte que should; might apenas mostra uma possibilidade.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The main is a database overload at peak hours."
"The impact is high, because it cause downtime."
"We optimize the query this week."
"We prepare a contingency plan."
"The is an old query that is too slow."
"If the rollback is not enough, I will the issue."

Stage 2: Put the Risk Report in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a clear risk report in the correct order.

?Then explain the root cause, the real reason behind the problem.
?First, name the main risk clearly.
?Finally, say when you must escalate and ask the team to review the report.
?Next, describe its likelihood and its impact.
?After that, present your mitigation and a contingency plan.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a calm, clear tone.

The likelihood is medium, but the impact is high.
A probabilidade é média, mas o impacto é alto.
We should optimize the query this week.
Nós deveríamos otimizar a consulta esta semana.
We must prepare a contingency plan.
Nós precisamos preparar um plano de contingência.
If the rollback is not enough, I will escalate the issue.
Se a reversão não for suficiente, eu vou escalar o problema.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each risk situation.

You want to say that downtime is possible, but not certain. You say:
A "The overload might cause downtime at peak hours."
B "The overload must cause downtime for sure."
C "There is no risk of downtime at all."
You recommend a first step, but it is not mandatory yet. You say:
A "We must to optimize the query."
B "We should optimize the query this week."
C "Optimize the query or you are fired."
The contingency plan is not optional for the launch. You say:
A "We must prepare a contingency plan before launch."
B "We might prepare a plan someday, maybe."
C "A contingency plan is not important."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a risk in one of your real IT projects. Record a short risk assessment out loud: name the main risk, describe its likelihood and impact, explain the root cause, then propose a mitigation (should), a contingency plan (must), and say when you might need a rollback or to escalate the issue.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1The main risk is a database overload at peak hours.O principal risco é uma sobrecarga do banco de dados nos horários de pico.
2The likelihood is medium, but the impact is high.A probabilidade é média, mas o impacto é alto.
3We should optimize the query this week.Nós deveríamos otimizar a consulta esta semana.
4We must prepare a contingency plan.Nós precisamos preparar um plano de contingência.
5If the rollback is not enough, I will escalate the issue.Se a reversão não for suficiente, eu vou escalar o problema.
Aula 10 -- Pre-class

The International Call -- Cross-Cultural Communication

Leading a call across time zones with clear, polite language: indirect questions and clarifying phrases to prevent misunderstandings. New vocabulary: clarify, misunderstanding, paraphrase, tone, jargon, rapport, acronym, overlap.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Clarify -- to make something clearer or explain it better
"Could you clarify what you mean by end of day?" (Esclarecer)
Misunderstanding -- a situation where people understand something incorrectly
"A small misunderstanding delayed the whole release." (Mal-entendido)
Paraphrase -- to say the same idea again in different, simpler words
"Let me paraphrase that to check I understood." (Parafrasear)
Tone -- the feeling or attitude that your words express
"A polite tone keeps an international call friendly." (Tom)
Jargon -- special technical words that one group or field uses
"Avoid jargon when the client is from another field." (Jargão)
Rapport -- a friendly, trusting connection between people
"Small talk at the start builds rapport." (Sintonia)
Acronym -- a short word made from the first letters of other words
"ASAP is an acronym for as soon as possible." (Sigla)
Overlap -- a shared period when two time zones are both working
"We only have a two-hour overlap with the Tokyo team." (Sobreposição)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Clarify
Misunderstanding
Paraphrase
Tone
Jargon
Rapport
Acronym
Overlap

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Rafael is leading a call with colleagues in three time zones. He knows they only have a short overlap, so a clear, polite tone matters. To avoid a misunderstanding, he asks indirect questions instead of direct ones. He does not say "When does the call start?"; he says, "Could you tell me when the call starts?" He does not say "Where is the client based?"; he says, "Do you know where the client is based?" When a point is complex, he stops and offers to paraphrase it in simpler words. He also avoids jargon and explains every acronym, because the new client is not technical. "I was wondering if we could begin with five minutes of small talk," he adds, to build rapport. Notice the pattern: after an introducer like "could you tell me" or "do you know", he keeps statement word order and drops "do" and "does".

1. Why does Rafael use a clear, polite tone and short messages?
A The teams share only a short overlap and he wants to avoid a misunderstanding.
B He wants to use as much jargon as possible.
C He has the whole day with every team.
2. How does he ask about the start time?
A "When does the call start?"
B "Could you tell me when the call starts?"
C "Could you tell me when does the call start?"
3. What does he do when a point is complex?
A He uses more acronyms to sound expert.
B He ends the call immediately.
C He stops and paraphrases it in simpler words.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Indirect Questions

GRAMMAR

Usamos perguntas indiretas para soar mais educados e evitar mal-entendidos em chamadas internacionais. / We use indirect questions to sound more polite and to avoid misunderstandings on international calls.

Direct / DiretaIndirect / Indireta
When does the call start?Could you tell me when the call starts?
Where is the client based?Do you know where the client is based?
Can we move the meeting?I was wondering if we could move the meeting.

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de um introdutor como could you tell me, do you know ou I was wondering, use a ordem de afirmação (sujeito + verbo) e não use do/does/did: "Could you tell me when the call starts?" (não "when does the call start"). Para perguntas de sim/não, use if: "I was wondering if we could move it."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Could you what you mean by end of day?"
"Could you tell me when the call ?"
"Do you know where the client based?"
"I was if we could move the meeting."
"Let me that to check I understood."
"We only have a two-hour with the Tokyo team."

Stage 2: Put the Call Opening in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a clear international call opening in the correct order.

?Then avoid jargon and acronyms, and keep a calm, friendly tone.
?First, set a simple rule: ask people to stop you if anything is not clear.
?Finally, use indirect questions, because they sound more polite and build rapport.
?Next, check the time zones and agree on the short overlap you share.
?After that, paraphrase complex points in simpler words to prevent a misunderstanding.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a calm, polite tone.

Could you tell me what time the call starts for you?
Você poderia me dizer que horas a chamada começa para você?
I was wondering if we could clarify two points.
Eu queria saber se poderíamos esclarecer dois pontos.
Let me paraphrase that to avoid any misunderstanding.
Deixe-me parafrasear isso para evitar qualquer mal-entendido.
We only have a short overlap, so let us keep a friendly tone.
Nós temos apenas uma curta sobreposição, então vamos manter um tom amigável.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each call situation.

You want to ask politely when the client is available. You say:
A "Could you tell me when the client is available?"
B "Could you tell me when is the client available?"
C "When client available, you tell?"
You did not understand a point and want to avoid a misunderstanding. You say:
A "You are wrong, say it again."
B "Sorry, could you clarify what you mean?"
C "Never mind, it does not matter."
The client is not technical. To keep a friendly tone, you should:
A Use as much jargon and as many acronyms as possible.
B Avoid jargon and paraphrase technical words in simpler English.
C Speak only in your own language.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are opening a call with colleagues in three different time zones. Record a short opening out loud: greet the team, mention the overlap you share, set one rule to avoid misunderstandings, and use at least two indirect questions (Could you tell me...? / I was wondering if...?) to clarify the agenda. Keep a calm, friendly tone.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1Could you tell me what time the call starts for you?Você poderia me dizer que horas a chamada começa para você?
2I was wondering if we could clarify two points.Eu queria saber se poderíamos esclarecer dois pontos.
3Let me paraphrase that to avoid a misunderstanding.Deixe-me parafrasear isso para evitar um mal-entendido.
4We only have a short overlap, so let us be quick.Nós temos apenas uma curta sobreposição, então vamos ser rápidos.
5Sorry, could you explain what that acronym means?Desculpe, você poderia explicar o que essa sigla significa?
Aula 11 -- Pre-class

Project Proposal -- Pitching Ideas to Leadership

Pitching a project idea to leadership and describing your plan with future forms: will for decisions and predictions, be going to for plans already decided. New vocabulary: pitch, buy-in, rationale, pilot, allocate, initiative, greenlight, headcount.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Pitch -- to present an idea in a short, persuasive way
"I will pitch the project to leadership on Friday." (Apresentar a ideia)
Buy-in -- agreement and support from the people who decide
"We need buy-in from the directors before we start." (Adesão)
Rationale -- the reasons and logic behind a decision or plan
"Let me explain the rationale for this proposal." (Justificativa)
Pilot -- a small first test of a project before the full launch
"We are going to run a pilot with one team first." (Projeto-piloto)
Allocate -- to give a resource, like money or people, to a task
"I only need to allocate two developers for one month." (Alocar)
Initiative -- a new plan or effort to improve or solve something
"This initiative will save the team ten hours a week." (Iniciativa)
Greenlight -- to give official approval to start something
"Leadership will greenlight the pilot this quarter." (Sinal verde)
Headcount -- the number of people on a team
"The headcount stays the same during the pilot." (Quadro de pessoal)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Pitch
Buy-in
Rationale
Pilot
Allocate
Initiative
Greenlight
Headcount

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Rafael is preparing to pitch an initiative to leadership. His rationale is clear: the manual reports are slow, so an automated tool will help. When he describes a plan he has already decided, he uses be going to: "We are going to run a small pilot with one team." When he makes a prediction or a promise in the moment, he uses will: "This will save us ten hours a week," and "I will share the results next month." He explains that he only needs to allocate two developers, so the headcount stays the same. He hopes for buy-in from the directors so they can greenlight the pilot this quarter. Notice the pattern: be going to for plans decided before now, and will for decisions made now, promises, and predictions.

1. Which form does Rafael use for a plan he has already decided?
A be going to ("We are going to run a pilot.")
B will ("We will run a pilot.")
C the past simple ("We ran a pilot.")
2. How does he make a prediction or a promise in the moment?
A "This is going to saving us time."
B "This will save us ten hours a week."
C "This saved us ten hours a week."
3. Why does the headcount stay the same?
A Because he is going to hire five new people.
B Because leadership declined the buy-in.
C Because he only needs to allocate two developers he already has.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Will vs. Be Going To

GRAMMAR

Usamos be going to para planos já decididos e previsões com evidência; usamos will para decisões tomadas na hora, promessas e previsões de opinião. / We use be going to for plans already decided and predictions with evidence; we use will for on-the-spot decisions, promises, and opinion predictions.

Use / UsoForm / FormaExample
Plan already decided / Plano já decididoam/is/are going to + verb"We are going to run a pilot."
Decision now / Promise / Decisão na horawill + verb"I will send you the proposal today."
Prediction / Previsãowill (opinion) / going to (evidence)"It will save time." / "These numbers show it is going to work."

Key point / Ponto-chave: use going to quando o plano já foi decidido antes de falar ("We are going to launch in March"); use will para uma decisão tomada no momento, uma promessa ou uma previsão ("I will help you"). Nunca junte os dois: diga "We are going to launch", não "We will going to launch".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"On Friday I will the project to leadership."
"We need from the directors before we start."
"Let me explain the for this proposal."
"We are going to run a small with one team."
"I only need to two developers for one month."
"If the numbers are good, leadership will the pilot."

Stage 2: Put the Pitch in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a strong project pitch in the correct order.

?Then present your plan and say what you are going to do, step by step.
?First, state the problem clearly, so leadership sees why it matters.
?Finally, ask clearly for the greenlight and offer to share the first results.
?Next, give your rationale with simple numbers and evidence.
?After that, propose a small pilot first, because it lowers the risk and builds buy-in.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a clear, confident tone.

We are going to run a small pilot with one team first.
Nós vamos rodar um pequeno projeto-piloto com uma equipe primeiro.
This initiative will save the team ten hours a week.
Esta iniciativa vai economizar dez horas por semana para a equipe.
Let me explain the rationale for this proposal.
Deixe-me explicar a justificativa para esta proposta.
With your buy-in, we can greenlight the pilot this quarter.
Com a sua adesão, podemos dar sinal verde ao piloto neste trimestre.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each pitch situation.

You already decided to launch the dashboard in March. You say:
A "We are going to launch the dashboard in March."
B "We will going to launch the dashboard in March."
C "We launch the dashboard in March yesterday."
A director asks for the report and you decide to send it right now. You say:
A "I am going to sent it last week."
B "No problem, I will send it right now."
C "I will sending it right now."
You want approval to start a small test before the full launch. You ask for:
A a bigger headcount and more budget for everyone.
B the greenlight to run a short pilot first.
C a full rollout with no rationale.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are pitching a real idea to leadership. Record a short pitch out loud: state the problem, give your rationale, describe your plan with "be going to", propose a small pilot first, and make one promise or prediction with "will". Ask clearly for the greenlight at the end.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1Let me pitch a small initiative that will save us time.Deixe-me apresentar uma pequena iniciativa que vai nos economizar tempo.
2We are going to start with a short pilot first.Nós vamos começar com um pequeno projeto-piloto primeiro.
3Let me explain the rationale for this proposal.Deixe-me explicar a justificativa para esta proposta.
4I only need to allocate two developers for one month.Eu só preciso alocar dois desenvolvedores por um mês.
5With your buy-in, we can greenlight the pilot this quarter.Com a sua adesão, podemos dar sinal verde ao piloto neste trimestre.
Aula 12 -- Pre-class

Quarterly Review -- Summarizing Results

Reading a real quarterly project report and summarizing results with the present perfect (We have moved, It has reached, The result has exceeded our target). New vocabulary: target, metric, on track, exceed, deliverable, setback, recap, progress, objective, achievement.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Target -- a result or number you plan and aim to reach
"We set a target of forty systems in the cloud." (Meta)
Metric -- a number you measure to track success
"Our most important metric is customer satisfaction." (Métrica)
On track -- going well and likely to reach the goal on time
"The project is still on track." (No caminho certo)
Exceed -- to do more than the target you set
"That result has exceeded our goal." (Superar / exceder)
Deliverable -- a finished result or piece of work you deliver
"Two key deliverables have gone live." (Entrega)
Setback -- a problem that slows your progress for a while
"We have had one setback this quarter." (Revés / contratempo)
Recap -- a short summary of the main points or results
"Let me give you a quick recap of the quarter." (Resumo)
Progress -- forward movement; getting closer to the goal
"The team has made real progress." (Progresso)
Objective -- the main goal you are working to achieve
"The migration has been our main objective." (Objetivo)
Achievement -- a good result you have worked hard to reach
"That is a real achievement for the team." (Conquista)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Target
Metric
Exceed
Setback
Recap
Progress
Objective
Achievement

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Rafael is getting ready for the quarterly review. He opens with a quick recap of the team's main objective: the Banco Atlas migration. "So far, we have made real progress," he writes. "We have moved thirty-eight of the forty systems, and the project is still on track." Then he shares the numbers. The team set a target of forty systems, and two key deliverables have gone live this quarter. The most important metric, customer satisfaction, has reached eighty-two percent, so it has exceeded the goal. He is honest about the setback too: a security test found a problem, so the rollout has slipped by two weeks. "Even so, the team has made strong progress, and the client has noticed." He uses the present perfect for every result, because each one connects the past to now.

1. What has happened to customer satisfaction?
A It has dropped below the target.
B It has reached 82% and exceeded the goal.
C The team has not measured it yet.
2. How many systems has the team moved so far?
A Thirty-eight of the forty.
B All forty systems.
C Only two systems.
3. What setback does Rafael report?
A The team has lost the client.
B The budget has run out.
C A security problem; the rollout has slipped by two weeks.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Present Perfect

GRAMMAR

Usamos o present perfect para falar de resultados e mudanças até AGORA, sem dizer o momento exato. / We use the present perfect for results and changes up to NOW, without saying the exact time.

Form / FormaStructureExample / Exemplo
Affirmative / Afirmativohave / has + past participleWe have moved 38 systems.
Third person / 3ª pessoahas + past participleIt has reached 82%.
Negative / Negativohave / has notThe quarter has not been perfect.
Question / PerguntaHave / Has + subject + participleHave you finished the report yet?

Key point / Ponto-chave: use o present perfect para resultados até agora, com palavras como so far, already, yet e this quarter. Com um tempo passado fechado (last week, yesterday), use o past simple: "We moved 38 systems last week." Depois de have / has, use o particípio passado (gone, não went).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"So far, we have our satisfaction target."
"Two deliverables have live this quarter."
"Our main is the Banco Atlas migration."
"We have had one : a security problem."
"The result has our target."

Stage 2: Put the Recap in Order

Order

Put the steps of a clear quarterly recap in the correct order.

?Then, point out the metric that has exceeded its target.
?First, state the main objective of the quarter.
?Finally, give the objective for next quarter.
?Next, share the key metrics and their targets.
?After that, be honest about one setback.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a calm, confident tone.

We have moved thirty-eight of the forty systems.
Nós já migramos trinta e oito dos quarenta sistemas.
Customer satisfaction has reached eighty-two percent.
A satisfação do cliente chegou a oitenta e dois por cento.
We have had one setback, but the team has made real progress.
Tivemos um contratempo, mas o time fez um progresso real.
Have you finished the quarterly report yet?
Você já terminou o relatório trimestral?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each review situation.

The board asks for a quick update on the migration. The best answer is:
A "We are on track. We have moved 38 of the 40 systems so far."
B "We move 38 systems every day."
C "We have moved all the systems last week."
You want to report a metric that beat its goal. You say:
A "Satisfaction is exceed the target."
B "Customer satisfaction has exceeded its target."
C "Customer satisfaction has exceeded its target yesterday."
A colleague asks if the report is ready. The most accurate answer is:
A "I have finished it last Monday."
B "Not yet. I have not added the setback section yet."
C "I am finish the report now."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are presenting your last quarter to the board. Record a short recap out loud: name your main objective, share one metric that has reached or exceeded its target, admit one setback, and say your objective for next quarter.
