Use the present perfect for the results (we have reached, it has exceeded, the rollout has slipped).

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1Let me give you a quick recap of the quarter.Deixa eu te dar um resumo rápido do trimestre.
2We are on track. We have moved 38 of the 40 systems.Estamos no caminho certo. Já migramos 38 dos 40 sistemas.
3Customer satisfaction has reached 82% and exceeded the target.A satisfação do cliente chegou a 82% e superou a meta.
4We have had one setback, but the team has made real progress.Tivemos um contratempo, mas o time fez um progresso real.
5Next quarter, our objective is to finish the migration.No próximo trimestre, nosso objetivo é terminar a migração.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

Onboarding a New Vendor -- Contract Discussion

Reviewing and discussing a vendor contract before you sign off: the second conditional to talk about hypothetical situations (If the vendor missed the deadline, we would apply a penalty). New vocabulary: warranty, amendment, penalty, liability, comply, party, breach, sign off.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Warranty -- a written promise that the work will meet a standard
"The warranty covers any defect for twelve months." (Garantia)
Amendment -- a written change added to a contract
"Any new work needs a written amendment both sides sign." (Aditivo / emenda)
Penalty -- a payment the vendor owes if they break a rule
"There is a penalty for every week of delay." (Multa / penalidade)
Liability -- legal responsibility for damage or loss
"The liability clause limits our risk in a data breach." (Responsabilidade)
Comply -- to follow the rules or terms of an agreement
"The vendor must comply with the ninety-day deadline." (Cumprir)
Party -- one of the two sides that sign a contract
"Any amendment must be signed by both parties." (Parte)
Breach -- breaking a contract or a security rule
"A data breach would trigger the liability clause." (Violação / quebra)
Sign off -- to give final approval to a document or contract
"We only sign off when both parties agree on the terms." (Aprovar / dar o aval)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Warranty
Amendment
Penalty
Liability
Comply
Party
Breach
Sign off

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Rafael is reviewing a new vendor contract before he and his legal team sign off. Overall, the terms look fair, but he wants to test a few clauses with hypothetical questions. He looks at the scope first: "If the scope changed later, we would need a written amendment." Then he checks the deadline: the vendor must comply with ninety days, and "if the vendor missed that deadline, we would apply a penalty." Finally, he reads the liability clause: "If a data breach happened, our exposure would be limited to the contract value." He is not comfortable with that cap, so he tells his team: "If I were you, I would ask for a higher limit." He uses the second conditional to explore situations that are not real now, but possible.

1. When does Rafael want to sign off on the contract?
A Immediately, before anyone reviews it.
B After he and the legal team review the clauses.
C Never, because the terms are unfair.
2. What would happen if the vendor missed the deadline?
A They would apply a penalty.
B They would extend the scope for free.
C Nothing would change in the contract.
3. What does Rafael say about the liability cap?
A "The cap is perfect, sign it now."
B "We do not need a liability clause."
C "If I were you, I would ask for a higher limit."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Second Conditional

GRAMMAR

Usamos o segundo condicional para falar de situações hipotéticas ou improváveis no presente ou futuro. / We use the second conditional to talk about hypothetical or unlikely situations in the present or future.

Part / ParteStructureExample / Exemplo
If-clause / CondiçãoIf + past simpleIf the vendor missed the deadline,
Result / Resultadowould + base verbwe would apply a penalty.
Negative / Negativowould not (wouldn't)we wouldn't sign without legal review.
Advice / ConselhoIf I were you, I would...If I were you, I would ask for a higher limit.

Key point / Ponto-chave: o segundo condicional usa past simple na condição (não é passado real) e would + verbo no resultado. Com o verbo to be, use were para todas as pessoas: If I were you.... É perfeito para negociar contratos sem se comprometer ainda.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If the vendor missed the deadline, we would apply a ."
"The vendor must with the ninety-day deadline."
"If a data breach happened, the clause would protect us."
"We only off when both parties agree."
"Overall, the of the contract look fair."
"If I you, I would ask for a higher limit."

Stage 2: Put the Review in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a smart contract review in the correct order.

?Then, look at the liability clause and check the cap.
?First, read the whole contract and check the scope.
?Finally, send your comments to legal and only then sign off.
?Next, find the deadline and the penalty for late delivery.
?After that, list every clause you want to change.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a calm, professional tone.

Overall, the terms of the contract look fair.
No geral, os termos do contrato parecem justos.
If the vendor missed the deadline, we would apply a penalty.
Se o fornecedor perdesse o prazo, nós aplicaríamos uma multa.
If I were you, I would ask for a higher liability limit.
Se eu fosse você, eu pediria um limite de responsabilidade mais alto.
We only sign off when both parties agree on the terms.
Só damos o aval quando as duas partes concordam com os termos.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each contract situation.

You want to explore what happens if the vendor delivers late. The best hypothetical question is:
A "If the vendor missed the deadline, what penalty would apply?"
B "When the vendor missed the deadline yesterday?"
C "The vendor will miss the deadline for sure."
You give a colleague advice about the liability cap. You say:
A "If I was you, I will sign it now."
B "If I were you, I would ask for a higher limit."
C "You signed it, so it is fine."
Legal asks if the contract is ready. The most accurate answer is:
A "I already signed off without reading it."
B "Not yet. We sign off only after both parties agree on the clauses."
C "There are no terms in this contract."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are reviewing a new vendor contract for your team. Record a short summary out loud: say if the terms look fair, name one clause you would change, and use the second conditional to explain what would happen if the vendor broke that clause (for example, the penalty or the liability cap).

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1Overall, the terms of the contract look fair.No geral, os termos do contrato parecem justos.
2If the vendor missed the deadline, we would apply a penalty.Se o fornecedor perdesse o prazo, nós aplicaríamos uma multa.
3The vendor must comply with the ninety-day deadline.O fornecedor deve cumprir o prazo de noventa dias.
4If I were you, I would ask for a higher liability limit.Se eu fosse você, eu pediria um limite de responsabilidade mais alto.
5We only sign off when both parties agree on the terms.Só damos o aval quando as duas partes concordam com os termos.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

Crisis Communication -- When Things Go Wrong

Communicating during a crisis: the past continuous to describe actions in progress in the past (Customers were trying to check out when the system failed). New vocabulary: incident, outage, severity, war room, status page, remediate, disruption, postmortem.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Incident -- an unplanned event that interrupts a service
"We opened a war room as soon as the incident started." (Incidente / ocorrência)
Outage -- a period when a service is down or unavailable
"The outage lasted about an hour before we restored it." (Indisponibilidade / queda)
Severity -- how serious or urgent a problem is
"We set the severity to one because customers were affected." (Gravidade / severidade)
War room -- a focused meeting where the team fixes a crisis
"We opened a war room and called the on-call engineer." (Sala de crise)
Status page -- a public page that shows if a service is up or down
"I updated the status page so customers knew about the outage." (Página de status)
Remediate -- to fix a problem and stop further damage
"We remediated the issue by restarting the service." (Sanar / corrigir)
Disruption -- an interruption that stops normal work or service
"The disruption affected about two thousand customers." (Interrupção / transtorno)
Postmortem -- a review after an incident to learn and prevent it
"In the postmortem, we found the root cause and wrote action items." (Análise pós-incidente)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Incident
Outage
Severity
War room
Status page
Remediate
Disruption
Postmortem

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Last night, Rafael's payment service had an incident. At about eleven p.m., while most of the team was sleeping, the service went down. Customers were trying to check out, but the system was returning errors. Rafael opened a war room and set the severity to one. When the on-call engineer joined, the database was running out of memory. While the team was remediating the problem, Rafael updated the status page so customers knew about the outage. The disruption lasted about an hour. Today, the team will run a postmortem to find the root cause. Rafael uses the past continuous to describe what was happening at each moment during the crisis.

1. What were the customers doing when the system failed?
A They were sleeping like the team.
B They were trying to check out.
C They were running the postmortem.
2. What was happening when the on-call engineer joined?
A The database was running out of memory.
B The service was already fully fixed.
C The status page was offline.
3. Why will the team run a postmortem?
A To start a new outage.
B To raise the severity again.
C To find the root cause and prevent it next time.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Past Continuous

GRAMMAR

Usamos o past continuous para descrever uma ação em progresso em um momento do passado, muitas vezes interrompida por outra ação. / We use the past continuous to describe an action in progress at a moment in the past, often interrupted by another action.

Part / ParteStructureExample / Exemplo
Affirmative / Afirmativowas / were + verb-ingthe service was returning errors.
Plural / youwere + verb-ingcustomers were trying to check out.
Negative / Negativowasn't / weren't + verb-ingwe weren't expecting an outage.
Interrupted / Interrompidopast continuous + when + past simpleI was watching the dashboard when the alert fired.

Key point / Ponto-chave: o past continuous usa was/were + verbo-ing para uma ação em progresso no passado. Use was com I/he/she/it e were com you/we/they. Uma ação longa (past continuous) costuma ser interrompida por uma curta (past simple) ligada por when ou while. É perfeito para narrar o que estava acontecendo durante um incidente.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Customers were trying to check out when the began."
"We set the to one because customers were affected."
"While we were fixing it, I updated the status ."
"We the issue by restarting the service."
"At eleven p.m., the service returning errors."
"Tomorrow we run a to find the root cause."

Stage 2: Put the Response in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a calm incident response in the correct order.

?Then, work with the on-call engineer to remediate the problem.
?First, confirm the incident is real and check how many customers it affects.
?Finally, run a postmortem and write action items to prevent it.
?Next, open a war room and set the severity.
?After that, update the status page with a clear, honest message.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a calm, professional tone.

We are investigating an incident with the payment service.
Estamos investigando um incidente no serviço de pagamento.
Customers were trying to check out when the system failed.
Os clientes estavam tentando finalizar a compra quando o sistema falhou.
We opened a war room and set the severity to one.
Abrimos uma sala de crise e definimos a gravidade como nível um.
We will run a postmortem to prevent this disruption next time.
Vamos fazer uma análise pós-incidente para evitar essa interrupção da próxima vez.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each crisis situation.

You want to describe what was happening at the start of the incident. The best sentence is:
A "Customers were trying to check out, but the service was returning errors."
B "Customers trying to check out, but the service returning errors."
C "Customers will try to check out tomorrow."
A customer asks why the site was down. The clearest, honest reply is:
A "Nothing happened, the site was fine."
B "We had an outage. We remediated it, and the service is now restored."
C "It is your internet, not our problem."
Your manager asks what comes next after the incident. The best answer is:
A "Nothing, the outage is over, so we are done."
B "We will run a postmortem to find the root cause and prevent it next time."
C "We will lower the severity and ignore it."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a service you manage just went down. Record a short incident update out loud: say what was happening when the outage started, what severity you set, how you remediated it, and what the postmortem will cover. Use the past continuous to describe the moment of the crisis.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1We are investigating an incident with the payment service.Estamos investigando um incidente no serviço de pagamento.
2Customers were trying to check out when the system failed.Os clientes estavam tentando finalizar a compra quando o sistema falhou.
3We opened a war room and set the severity to one.Abrimos uma sala de crise e definimos a gravidade como nível um.
4We remediated the issue, and the outage is now over.Sanamos o problema, e a indisponibilidade já acabou.
5We will run a postmortem to prevent this disruption next time.Vamos fazer uma análise pós-incidente para evitar essa interrupção da próxima vez.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

The Job Interview -- Telling Your Story

Telling your professional story in a job interview: the present perfect for experience up to now (I have led eight releases) and the past simple for a finished moment (Last year I led the response). New vocabulary: candidate, recruiter, resume, shortlist, strengths, qualification, expertise, track record.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Candidate -- a person who is applying for a job
"As a candidate, I prepared three stories about my projects." (Candidato)
Recruiter -- the person who manages the hiring process
"The recruiter called to say I was on the shortlist." (Recrutador)
Resume -- a short document of your experience and skills
"I updated my resume before I applied for the role." (Currículo)
Shortlist -- the small group of finalists for a job
"They moved me to the shortlist of three candidates." (Lista de finalistas)
Strengths -- the qualities and skills you are very good at
"My strengths are clear communication and staying calm." (Pontos fortes)
Qualification -- a skill or degree that makes you right for a job
"My main qualification is six years of delivery experience." (Qualificação)
Expertise -- a high level of skill in a specific area
"My expertise is leading teams through complex releases." (Expertise / domínio)
Track record -- your history of past results over time
"I have a strong track record of shipping on time." (Histórico de resultados)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Candidate
Recruiter
Resume
Shortlist
Strengths
Qualification
Expertise
Track record

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Rafael is a candidate for a senior project manager role. In the interview, he describes his experience up to now with the present perfect. He says, "I have worked in technology for twelve years, and I have led delivery teams for the last six. I have managed eight major releases, and I have never missed a launch date." Then the recruiter asks for a real example, so Rafael switches to the past simple, because the time is finished: "Three years ago, I joined the company to fix a late project. I rebuilt the plan, and we shipped the product on time. Last year, we had a serious outage, and I led the response." His clear track record and his strengths put him on the shortlist.

1. Why does Rafael use the present perfect at the start?
A Because he is talking about tomorrow.
B Because he is describing his experience up to now, with no finished time.
C Because he is asking a question.
2. Why does he switch to the past simple for the example?
A Because the time is finished (three years ago, last year).
B Because the recruiter speaks English.
C Because the past simple is more formal.
3. What put Rafael on the shortlist?
A A long resume with no examples.
B Only his salary expectations.
C His clear track record and his strengths.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect vs Past Simple

GRAMMAR

Usamos o present perfect para a experiência até agora (sem tempo terminado) e o past simple para um momento terminado com tempo definido. / We use the present perfect for experience up to now (no finished time) and the past simple for a finished moment with a definite time.

Tense / TempoUse it for / Usar paraExample / Exemplo
Present perfectexperiência até agora, sem tempo terminadoI have led eight releases.
Past simplemomento terminado com tempo definidoLast year I led the response.
Present perfect signalsever, never, for, since, so farI have never missed a launch.
Past simple signalsyesterday, last year, ago, in 2020Three years ago I joined the team.

Key point / Ponto-chave: use o present perfect (have/has + particípio) para a experiência geral da sua carreira até agora, com palavras como ever, never, for, since. No momento em que você diz quando (last year, in 2022, three years ago), troque para o past simple. É o erro mais comum em entrevista: "Last year I have led..." está errado; o certo é "Last year I led...".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I have worked in technology twelve years."
"Three years , I joined this company."
"The put me on the shortlist."
"I have a strong of shipping on time."
"My main is six years of experience."
"Last year, I the incident response."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of preparing for an interview in the correct order.

?Then, in the interview, give clear examples with real numbers.
?First, research the company and the role to show why you fit.
?Finally, send a thank-you message and confirm the next step.
?Next, prepare three short stories that prove your strengths.
?After that, ask two or three smart questions about the team.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a confident, professional tone.

I have worked in technology for twelve years.
Trabalho com tecnologia há doze anos.
I have managed eight releases and never missed a launch.
Já gerenciei oito lançamentos e nunca perdi uma data.
Last year, I led a serious incident from start to finish.
No ano passado, liderei um incidente sério do início ao fim.
I am a strong candidate with the right qualification.
Sou um candidato forte, com a qualificação certa.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each interview situation.

The interviewer asks about your experience up to now. The best sentence is:
A "I have led delivery teams for six years, and I have never missed a launch."
B "Last year I have led delivery teams for six years."
C "I will lead delivery teams since six years."
You want to give one finished example. The clearest answer is:
A "Three years ago, I have joined the company to fix a project."
B "Three years ago, I joined the company and fixed a late project."
C "I join the company three years ago to fix a project."
The recruiter asks why you are a strong candidate. The best answer is:
A "I have no real examples, but I am sure I am good."
B "I have the right qualification and a strong track record of results."
C "I do not know, the resume says everything."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are in a job interview. Record a short answer out loud: introduce yourself, describe your experience up to now using the present perfect, give one finished example using the past simple, and say why you are a strong candidate. Mix the two tenses correctly.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1I have worked in technology for twelve years.Trabalho com tecnologia há doze anos.
2I have managed eight releases, and I have never missed a launch.Já gerenciei oito lançamentos e nunca perdi uma data.
3Last year, I led a serious incident from start to finish.No ano passado, liderei um incidente sério do início ao fim.
4My strengths are clear communication and staying calm under pressure.Meus pontos fortes são comunicação clara e manter a calma sob pressão.
5I am a strong candidate with the right qualification and track record.Sou um candidato forte, com a qualificação e o histórico certos.
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

The Full Interview & Program Review

Running a full job interview from start to finish with the STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result), reviewing the present perfect for experience up to now and the past simple for a finished story. New vocabulary: rehearse, mindset, composure, walkthrough, articulate, anecdote, showcase, debrief.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Rehearse -- to practice something before you do it for real
"I rehearsed my interview answers the night before." (Ensaiar)
Mindset -- your attitude and way of thinking about a situation
"My mindset is to stay calm and own the outcome." (Mentalidade)
Composure -- the ability to stay calm and in control under pressure
"The director praised my composure during the hard questions." (Compostura / calma)
Walkthrough -- a clear, step-by-step explanation of how something was done
"I gave the director a walkthrough of the project from start to finish." (Explicação passo a passo)
Articulate -- to express an idea clearly and simply
"He articulates complex ideas in a simple way." (Expressar com clareza)
Anecdote -- a short, true story you tell to make a point
"I shared a quick anecdote about a project I rescued." (Caso / historinha real)
Showcase -- to present your skills or results clearly
"The interview is your chance to showcase your track record." (Evidenciar / exibir)
Debrief -- a short meeting to review something after it happened
"After the panel, the team had a debrief and made a decision." (Reunião de balanço)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Rehearse
Mindset
Composure
Walkthrough
Articulate
Anecdote
Showcase
Debrief

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

For the final interview, Rafael rehearsed his answers so he could keep his composure. He opened with his experience up to now in the present perfect: "I have led delivery teams for six years, and I have managed eight major releases." Then he gave the director a clear walkthrough of one project using the STAR method, in the past simple, because the story was finished: "When I joined, the release was eight weeks late. I rebuilt the plan, I cut the scope, and we shipped on time. The client renewed for two years." He articulated his ideas clearly and added a short anecdote to showcase his mindset. After a quick debrief, the panel decided to offer him the role.

1. Why does Rafael open with the present perfect?
A Because he is describing tomorrow.
B Because he is showcasing his experience up to now, with no finished time.
C Because the present perfect is more polite.
2. Why is the walkthrough in the past simple?
A Because it is one finished story (when I joined, I rebuilt, we shipped).
B Because the director is older.
C Because the past simple has no rules.
3. What helped Rafael keep his composure?
A He did not prepare at all.
B He rehearsed his answers before the interview.
C He read the resume out loud.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The STAR Method & Tenses

GRAMMAR

Use o STAR para estruturar a resposta: Situation, Task, Action, Result. Abra com a experiência até agora no present perfect e conte UMA história terminada no past simple. / Use STAR to structure your answer: Situation, Task, Action, Result. Open with your experience up to now in the present perfect, then tell ONE finished story in the past simple.

Step / PassoIt answers / RespondeExample / Exemplo
S -- Situationo contexto (onde você estava)The release was eight weeks late.
T -- Taskseu objetivo ou o problemaMy task was to get it back on track.
A -- Action (past simple)o que você FEZI rebuilt the plan and aligned the team.
R -- Result (com número)o desfechoWe shipped on time and the client renewed.

Key point / Ponto-chave: abra mostrando sua carreira até agora no present perfect ("I have led eight releases"); depois conte UMA história terminada no past simple seguindo o STAR. O Result sempre traz um número real (on time, two years), porque um número soa concreto. Evite respostas que são só opinião, sem história nem resultado.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I my answers the night before."
"The director praised my during the hard questions."
"Let me give you a of the project."
"My is to stay calm and own the outcome."
"The interview is your chance to your track record."
"After the panel, the team had a and made a decision."

Stage 2: Put the STAR Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the STAR steps of a strong answer in the correct order.

?Then describe the action: what did you actually do, step by step?
?First, set the situation: where were you and what was the context?
?Finally, add one short takeaway: what did you learn from it?
?Next, explain the task: what was your goal or the problem to solve?
?After that, share the result with a real number, like on time or two years.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a confident, professional tone.

Let me give you a walkthrough of one project, from start to finish.
Deixe-me explicar um projeto passo a passo, do início ao fim.
When I joined, the release was late. I rebuilt the plan and we shipped on time.
Quando entrei, o lançamento estava atrasado. Refiz o plano e entregamos no prazo.
My mindset is to stay calm, communicate clearly, and own the outcome.
Minha mentalidade é manter a calma, comunicar com clareza e assumir o resultado.
I rehearsed this answer, but I mean every word.
Ensaiei esta resposta, mas penso cada palavra.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each interview situation.

The director asks for a walkthrough of one project. The best opening is:
A "When I joined, the release was eight weeks late, so I rebuilt the plan."
B "When I joined, I have rebuilt the plan eight weeks late."
C "I am good with late projects, trust me."
You want a strong Result step. The clearest sentence is:
A "The result was good and everyone was happy."
B "We shipped on time, and the client renewed the contract for two years."
C "The result will be great next year."
The director asks how you keep your composure. The best answer is:
A "I never feel pressure, so it is easy."
B "My mindset is to stay calm, communicate clearly, and own the outcome."
C "I will rehearse it later, I promise."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are in the final round of a job interview. Record a full answer out loud: open with your experience up to now in the present perfect, give one STAR story in the past simple (situation, action, and a result with a real number), describe your mindset, and say why you are the right candidate.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1Let me give you a walkthrough of one project, from start to finish.Deixe-me explicar um projeto passo a passo, do início ao fim.
2When I joined, the release was late. I rebuilt the plan and we shipped on time.Quando entrei, o lançamento estava atrasado. Refiz o plano e entregamos no prazo.
3My mindset is to stay calm, communicate clearly, and own the outcome.Minha mentalidade é manter a calma, comunicar com clareza e assumir o resultado.
4I rehearsed this answer, but I mean every word.Ensaiei esta resposta, mas penso cada palavra.
5I am ready to lead from day one.Estou pronto para liderar desde o primeiro dia.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Leading the Handover & Documenting the Process

Explaining how a team works so the job runs without one person, using the passive voice in the present (the report is reviewed) and past (the build was tested), and naming the agent with by when it matters. New vocabulary: handover, document, procedure, approve, assign, monitor, maintain, standardize.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Handover -- passing your work, tasks, and knowledge to another person
"The handover is designed so the team continues without me." (Passagem / transição de trabalho)
Document -- to write something down clearly so others can follow it
"Every change is documented in the wiki before it goes live." (Documentar / registrar)
Procedure -- a fixed set of steps for doing a task the same way every time
"The procedure is simple and it is followed by everyone." (Procedimento)
Approve -- to officially accept something so it can go forward
"The build is only approved when every check passes." (Aprovar)
Assign -- to give a task or ticket to a specific person
"Tickets are assigned at the Monday stand-up." (Atribuir / designar)
Monitor -- to watch a system or task over time to catch problems
"After release, the system is monitored for forty-eight hours." (Monitorar / acompanhar)
Maintain -- to keep something updated and in good working condition
"The runbook is maintained by everyone, not just me." (Manter / conservar atualizado)
Standardize -- to make something the same across teams, one agreed way
"The process was standardized across all three squads." (Padronizar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Handover
Document
Procedure
Approve
Assign
Monitor
Maintain
Standardize

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

On his first month as lead, Rafael wrote a clear handover so the team could run without him. In his process, every change is documented in the wiki, and the procedure is followed by everyone. Tickets are assigned at the stand-up, and the work is monitored on the board all week. The build is tested by the quality team and is approved only when every check passes. Last quarter the whole process was standardized across three squads, and incidents were cut in half. The runbook was written by the team, so it is maintained by everyone, not just Rafael.

1. Why does Rafael use the passive voice to describe the process?
A Because the passive is always more polite.
B Because the action matters more than the person at each step.
C Because he forgot who does the work.
2. Which sentence is in the PAST passive?
A "Every change is documented in the wiki."
B "The process was standardized across three squads."
C "Tickets are assigned at the stand-up."
3. Why does nothing depend on one person?
A Because only Rafael knows the steps.
B Because the runbook is maintained by everyone and the process is standardized.
C Because the team never documents anything.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Passive Voice

GRAMMAR

Use a voz passiva quando a AÇÃO importa mais que a pessoa, ou quando a pessoa é óbvia ou desconhecida. A forma é sempre be + past participle. Se quiser dizer quem fez, use by + pessoa. / Use the passive voice when the action matters more than the person. The form is always be + past participle. Add by + person only if you want to name who did it.

Form / FormaStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Presentam / is / are + past participleThe report is reviewed every Friday.
Present (plural)are + past participleTickets are assigned at the stand-up.
Pastwas / were + past participleThe build was tested before launch.
With the agent (by)be + past participle + by + personThe runbook was written by the team.

Key point / Ponto-chave: a passiva precisa SEMPRE do verbo be (is, are, was, were) + o past participle. Não esqueça o be: "The report reviewed" está errado; "The report is reviewed" está certo. Use by + pessoa só quando você quer nomear quem faz.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Every change is in the wiki."
"Tickets are at the stand-up."
"The build is only when every check passes."
"The system is after every release."
"Last quarter the process was across the squads."
"The runbook is by everyone, not just me."

Stage 2: Put the Handover Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of the release process in the correct order.

?Then the work is monitored on the board all week.
?First, the change is documented in the wiki before anything goes live.
?Finally, the new process is standardized, so it is maintained by everyone.
?Next, the ticket is assigned to an owner at the stand-up.
?After that, the build is tested and approved only when every check passes.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a clear, professional tone.

Every change is documented in the wiki before it goes live.
Toda mudança é documentada no wiki antes de entrar no ar.
Tickets are assigned at the stand-up, and the work is monitored all week.
Os tickets são atribuídos na reunião em pé, e o trabalho é monitorado a semana toda.
The build was tested by the quality team and approved.
A versão foi testada pela equipe de qualidade e aprovada.
The runbook is maintained by everyone, not just me.
O manual é mantido por todos, não só por mim.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each handover situation.

A new teammate asks how changes are handled. The clearest answer is:
A "Every change is documented in the wiki before it goes live."
B "Every change documented in the wiki."
C "Somebody, I think, writes it down maybe."
You want to describe a past result. The correct passive is:
A "Last quarter the process standardized across squads."
B "Last quarter the process was standardized across squads."
C "Last quarter the process is standardized across squads."
You want to name who keeps the runbook updated. The best sentence is:
A "The runbook maintains the team."
B "The runbook is maintained by the whole team."
C "The runbook is maintain by the team."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a new person joined your team today. Record a short handover out loud: explain each step of one process you own using the passive voice (present), add one past result with the past passive, and say who maintains it using by.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1Every change is documented in the wiki before it goes live.Toda mudança é documentada no wiki antes de entrar no ar.
2Tickets are assigned at the stand-up, and the work is monitored all week.Os tickets são atribuídos na reunião em pé, e o trabalho é monitorado a semana toda.
3The build is tested by the quality team and approved when every check passes.A versão é testada pela equipe de qualidade e aprovada quando toda verificação passa.
4Last quarter the process was standardized across all the squads.No trimestre passado, o processo foi padronizado em todas as equipes.
5The runbook is maintained by everyone, so nothing depends on one person.O manual é mantido por todos, então nada depende de uma só pessoa.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

Difficult Conversations & Giving Feedback

Giving clear, kind feedback to your team: address the behavior not the person, acknowledge what is true, ask for the perspective, and reassure. New grammar: reported speech (he said the release was late; she told me she would fix it). New vocabulary: feedback, address, acknowledge, behavior, perspective, defensive, reassure, accountable.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Feedback -- useful, honest information you give someone to help them grow
"Good feedback is not about blame; it is about helping someone grow." (Feedback / retorno)
Address -- to deal with a problem or topic directly, not avoid it
"Last week I had to address a real problem with a developer." (Tratar / abordar diretamente)
Acknowledge -- to accept and say openly that something is true
"I acknowledged that the workload had been heavy." (Reconhecer / admitir)
Behavior -- the way a person acts; what they do, not who they are
"Describe the behavior, never attack the person." (Comportamento)
Perspective -- a person's point of view; how they see a situation
"Then I asked for his perspective, because his side matters." (Perspectiva / ponto de vista)
Defensive -- reacting to protect yourself, so you stop listening
"When people feel respected, they do not become defensive." (Defensivo / na defensiva)
Reassure -- to make someone feel calmer and supported in a hard moment
"I reassured him that I was on his side." (Tranquilizar / dar segurança)
Accountable -- responsible; ready to own the result of your own work
"When people feel respected, they stay accountable and they change." (Responsável / que presta contas)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Feedback
Address
Acknowledge
Behavior
Perspective
Defensive
Reassure
Accountable

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Last week Rafael had to address a difficult problem with a developer. In their one-on-one, he stayed calm and started with the facts. He said the last two releases were late, and he acknowledged that the workload had been heavy. Then he asked for the developer's perspective. The developer told him the test environment had been broken for days, and that nobody had reported it. Rafael reassured him that he was on his side, so the developer did not become defensive. He said he would flag any blocker early, and Rafael focused on the behavior, not the person. The developer stayed accountable, and the hard conversation built trust instead of fear.

1. Why did the developer NOT become defensive?
A Because Rafael attacked the person, not the behavior.
B Because Rafael acknowledged the workload and reassured him he was on his side.
C Because Rafael avoided the problem.
2. Which sentence is correct reported speech?
A "He said me the releases are late."
B "He said the releases were late."
C "He told the releases were late."
3. What did the developer tell Rafael was the real cause?
A The test environment had been broken and nobody had reported it.
B He did not want to do the work.
C Rafael had given him too little feedback.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech

GRAMMAR

Para reportar o que alguém disse, o verbo geralmente RECUA um passo no tempo: present vira past, will vira would. Use say sem pessoa (he said that...) e tell com pessoa (he told me that...). / To report what someone said, the verb usually moves one step back in time: present becomes past, will becomes would. Use say with no person, and tell with a person.

Direct / DiretoReported / ReportadoExample / Exemplo
is / arewas / wereHe said the release was late.
present simplepast simpleShe told me she needed more time.
willwouldHe said he would fix it.
past simplepast perfect (had + p.p.)She told me the tests had passed.

Key point / Ponto-chave: use tell + pessoa (told me), nunca "said me". O verbo recua um passo: "He said me he is busy" está errado; "He told me he was busy" está certo. O "that" é opcional.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I would like to something honestly."
"I the workload has been heavy."
"Thank you for sharing your ."
"I want to you that I am on your side."
"Describe the , never attack the person."
"He told me the build broken."

Stage 2: Put the Feedback Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a good feedback conversation in the correct order.

?Then I asked for his perspective and listened to his side.
?First, I described the behavior with the facts, not the person.
?Finally, we agreed on a clear next step, and he said he would flag any blocker early.
?Next, I acknowledged that the workload had been heavy.
?After that, I reassured him that I was on his side.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a calm, respectful tone.

I would like to address something honestly.
Eu gostaria de tratar de uma coisa com sinceridade.
I acknowledge the workload has been heavy, and I want to reassure you.
Eu reconheço que a carga de trabalho tem sido pesada, e quero te tranquilizar.
He told me the environment had been broken, so the delay was not his fault.
Ele me disse que o ambiente tinha quebrado, então o atraso não foi culpa dele.
Describe the behavior, never attack the person.
Descreva o comportamento, nunca ataque a pessoa.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each feedback situation.

You want to open a difficult conversation without attacking the person. The best opener is:
A "I would like to address something honestly: the last two releases were late."
B "You are always slow and unreliable."
C "Never mind, it does not matter."
You report what a teammate said in the meeting. The correct sentence is:
A "She said me she will fix it."
B "She told me she would fix it."
C "She told she would fix it."
A teammate is getting defensive. The best thing to say is:
A "Stop making excuses."
B "I want to reassure you: I am on your side, and I value your perspective."
C "This is your problem, not mine."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of a real situation where you had to give difficult feedback. Record a short feedback conversation out loud: address the behavior with the facts, report one thing the person told you using reported speech (he told me..., she said...), reassure them, and agree on a clear next step.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1I would like to address something honestly.Eu gostaria de tratar de uma coisa com sinceridade.
2I acknowledge the workload has been heavy. Thank you for your perspective.Eu reconheço que a carga foi pesada. Obrigado pela sua perspectiva.
3I want to reassure you that I am on your side.Eu quero te tranquilizar: estou do seu lado.
4He told me the environment had been broken, so the delay was not his fault.Ele me disse que o ambiente tinha quebrado, então o atraso não foi culpa dele.
5Describe the behavior, never attack the person.Descreva o comportamento, nunca ataque a pessoa.
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Negotiating & Reaching Agreement

Getting to yes without losing the relationship: find common ground, use your leverage, set your conditions, make a concession, and settle on a win-win. New grammar: conditional conjunctions (unless / as long as / provided that). New vocabulary: leverage, concession, deal-breaker, win-win, common ground, bargain, settle, mutual.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Leverage -- an advantage or source of power that helps you in a deal
"My biggest leverage was our two-year commitment." (Vantagem / poder de barganha)
Concession -- something you give up so both sides can reach a deal
"In the end, we both made a concession." (Concessão)
Deal-breaker -- a condition so important that, without it, there is no deal
"I separated the deal-breakers from the small concessions." (Condição decisiva)
Win-win -- a result where both sides benefit, not just one
"I wanted a win-win, not a win for one side." (Ganha-ganha)
Common ground -- the points both sides already agree on
"A good negotiation is a search for common ground." (Terreno comum)
Bargain -- to discuss terms to get a better price or condition
"We bargained over the unit price for an hour." (Barganhar / negociar)
Settle -- to reach a final agreement that ends a discussion
"We did not just settle for less; we found a fair deal." (Fechar / chegar a acordo)
Mutual -- shared by both sides equally
"We found a deal with mutual benefit for both companies." (Mútuo)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Leverage
Concession
Deal-breaker
Win-win
Common ground
Bargain
Settle
Mutual

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Last month Rafael had to renew the contract with the main cloud vendor. He prepared carefully: he listed his deal-breakers and his small concessions, and he knew his main leverage was a two-year commitment. In the meeting, he started with the relationship and looked for common ground, because he wanted a win-win. Then he set his conditions. He said they would commit to more volume provided that the unit price dropped, and they could extend the term as long as the vendor improved the response time. He was also very clear about one thing: unless the vendor guaranteed the uptime, there would be no deal at all. After they bargained for a while, both sides made a concession and settled on a fair deal with mutual benefit.

1. Why did Rafael start with the relationship and common ground?
A Because he only cared about the lowest price.
B Because he wanted a win-win, a deal good for both sides.
C Because he wanted to end the partnership.
2. Which sentence sets a condition correctly?
A "We will commit provided that the price will drop."
B "We will commit provided that the price drops."
C "We will commit unless the price drops."
3. What did "unless the vendor guaranteed the uptime" mean for the deal?
A If the vendor did not guarantee uptime, there would be no deal.
B The uptime did not matter at all.
C The deal would happen no matter what.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Conditional Conjunctions

GRAMMAR

Numa negociação, você coloca condições para o acordo. as long as e provided that = só se (only if); unless = se não / a menos que (if not). Depois dessas conjunções, use o PRESENTE, nunca "will". / In a negotiation you set conditions for the deal. as long as and provided that = only if; unless = if not. After these conjunctions, use the present tense, not "will".

ConjunctionMeaning / SentidoExample / Exemplo
as long asonly if / só seWe can sign as long as you improve the SLA.
provided thatonly if / na condição de queWe will commit provided that the price drops.
unlessif not / a menos queUnless you guarantee uptime, we cannot agree.

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de unless, as long as e provided that, use o PRESENTE: "provided that you reduce the price" (não "will reduce"). Unless = se não; as long as / provided that = só se.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We cannot agree you guarantee the uptime."
"We will commit to more volume the price drops."
"I will accept the deal we keep the exit clause."
"Our two-year commitment is our main ."
"Let us start by finding some ."
"In the end, both sides made a ."

Stage 2: Put the Negotiation Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a good negotiation in the correct order.

?Then I opened with the relationship and made it clear I wanted a win-win.
?First, I listed what we wanted and separated the deal-breakers from the small concessions.
?Finally, we both made a concession and settled on a deal with mutual benefit.
?Next, I identified my main leverage, our two-year commitment.
?After that, I set my conditions using if, unless, and as long as.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a clear, confident tone.

Before the price, let us find some common ground.
Antes do preço, vamos achar um terreno comum.
We will commit to more volume provided that the unit price drops.
Vamos nos comprometer com mais volume desde que o preço unitário caia.
Unless you guarantee the uptime, we cannot agree.
A menos que vocês garantam o uptime, não podemos fechar.
Let us settle on a win-win with mutual benefit.
Vamos fechar num ganha-ganha com benefício mútuo.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each negotiation situation.

You want a longer contract, but only if the vendor lowers the price. The best sentence is:
A "We will sign a longer contract provided that you lower the price."
B "We will sign a longer contract provided that you will lower the price."
C "We will sign a longer contract unless you lower the price."
Uptime is a deal-breaker. The clearest way to say it is:
A "We can agree if you do not guarantee the uptime."
B "Unless you guarantee the uptime, we cannot agree."
C "We will agree as long as you do not guarantee the uptime."
You want to open the negotiation in a constructive way. The best opener is:
A "Your price is too high and you are wrong."
B "Before the price, let us find some common ground. I want a win-win."
C "Take it or leave it."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of a real deal you negotiate or want to negotiate (a vendor, a salary, a project budget). Record a short negotiation plan out loud: name your main leverage, name one deal-breaker, and set two conditions using "unless" and "provided that". Finish by saying what win-win you are aiming for.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1Before the price, let us find some common ground.Antes do preço, vamos achar um terreno comum.
2We will commit to more volume provided that the unit price drops.Vamos nos comprometer com mais volume desde que o preço caia.
3Unless you guarantee the uptime, we cannot agree.A menos que vocês garantam o uptime, não podemos fechar.
4I will accept the deal as long as we keep the exit clause.Eu aceito o acordo desde que a gente mantenha a cláusula de saída.
5Let us settle on a win-win with mutual benefit.Vamos fechar num ganha-ganha com benefício mútuo.
Aula 20 -- Pre-class

Closing the Deal -- Confirming the Final Terms

The final step of a deal: finalize the terms, confirm every detail, get the sign-off and signature, agree the effective date, and plan the kick-off. New grammar: question tags (..., didn't we? / ..., won't you?). New vocabulary: finalize, sign-off, term sheet, signature, effective date, kick-off, proceed, formalize.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Finalize -- to make the terms final and complete, ready to sign
"First, I had to finalize the terms of the contract." (Finalizar / concluir)
Sign-off -- the formal approval that lets a deal go ahead
"The day the sign-off came through, the deal was done." (Aprovação formal)
Term sheet -- a document listing the main agreed terms of a deal
"I went through every line of the term sheet." (Resumo dos termos)
Signature -- your name written to approve a document
"Legal prepared the agreement for signature." (Assinatura)
Effective date -- the day the contract officially starts to apply
"We set the effective date for the first of the month." (Data de vigência)
Kick-off -- the first meeting that starts a new project or contract
"We planned a short kick-off call to start the contract." (Reunião inicial)
Proceed -- to go ahead to the next step of a process
"Once both sides confirmed, we could proceed to signature." (Prosseguir)
Formalize -- to make an agreement official, usually in writing
"Our legal team could formalize the agreement." (Formalizar / oficializar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Finalize
Sign-off
Term sheet
Signature
Effective date
Kick-off
Proceed
Formalize

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

After Rafael and the vendor reached an agreement, the deal was not done yet. First he had to finalize the terms: he checked every line of the term sheet against what they had agreed. Then he confirmed the details on a call, using question tags. He said, "We agreed on the twelve-month term, didn't we?" and "You will keep the uptime guarantee, won't you?" The vendor confirmed each point. Once both sides agreed on the same terms, the legal team could formalize the agreement and prepare it for signature. They set the effective date for the first of the month, got the sign-off, and planned a short kick-off call so the project could proceed smoothly.

1. Why did Rafael use question tags on the call?
A To start a new negotiation from zero.
B To confirm the agreed details and get a clear yes before signing.
C To change the price at the last minute.
2. Which question tag is correct?
A "We agreed on the term, agreed we?"
B "We agreed on the term, didn't we?"
C "We agreed on the term, did we?"
3. What happened once both sides confirmed the same terms?
A Legal formalized the agreement and prepared it for signature.
B They cancelled the whole deal.
C They went back to bargaining over the price.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Question Tags

GRAMMAR

Para confirmar um detalhe, você faz uma afirmação e acrescenta uma pequena pergunta no fim: o question tag. Regra do espelho: frase POSITIVA leva tag NEGATIVO ("We agreed, didn't we?"); frase NEGATIVA leva tag POSITIVO ("We didn't change it, did we?"). O tag repete o verbo auxiliar (do/did, will, be) e o pronome. / To confirm a detail, you make a statement and add a short question at the end: the question tag. Mirror rule: a positive statement takes a negative tag; a negative statement takes a positive tag. The tag repeats the auxiliary verb and the pronoun.

Statement / FraseTagExample / Exemplo
Positive (past)didn't + pronounWe agreed on the term, didn't we?
Positive (will)won't + pronounYou will sign it, won't you?
Positive (be)isn't / aren't + pronounThe date is the first, isn't it?
Negativepositive auxiliary + pronounWe didn't change it, did we?

Key point / Ponto-chave: o tag é um espelho. Frase positiva -> tag negativo ("..., didn't we?"); frase negativa -> tag positivo ("..., did we?"). O tag repete o auxiliar (did, won't, isn't), nunca o verbo principal.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or tag. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We agreed on the final price, ?"
"You will send the signed copy, ?"
"Before we sign, we need to the terms."
"The deal goes ahead once we get the ."
"The contract starts on the ."
"Legal will the agreement and prepare it for signature."

Stage 2: Put the Closing Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of closing a deal in the correct order.

?Then our legal team formalized the agreement and prepared it for signature.
?First, I finalized the term sheet and checked every line against what we had agreed.
?Finally, we planned a short kick-off call to start the new contract smoothly.
?Next, I confirmed the key terms with the vendor using question tags.
?After that, both sides completed the sign-off and we set the effective date.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Focus on a clear, confident tone.

Before we sign, let us confirm the term sheet.
Antes de assinar, vamos confirmar o resumo dos termos.
We agreed on the twelve-month term, didn't we?
A gente concordou com o prazo de doze meses, né?
You will keep the uptime guarantee, won't you?
Vocês vão manter a garantia de uptime, não vão?
Once we have the sign-off, we proceed to the kick-off.
Assim que tivermos a aprovação, seguimos para a reunião inicial.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each closing situation.

You want to confirm that you both agreed on the price. The best sentence is:
A "We agreed on the price, didn't we?"
B "We agreed on the price, did we?"
C "We agreed on the price, agree we?"
The vendor will sign this week, and you want to confirm it. The clearest tag is:
A "You will sign this week, will you?"
B "You will sign this week, won't you?"
C "You will sign this week, don't you?"
All terms are confirmed. The best way to move forward is:
A "Let us start the negotiation again from zero."
B "Everything is confirmed, so legal can formalize it and we proceed to signature."
C "Let us change the price one more time."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of a real deal you close or want to close at work (a vendor contract, a project sign-off, a renewal). Record a short closing checklist out loud: name two terms you would confirm with question tags ("We agreed on..., didn't we?"), and describe the steps from sign-off and signature to the kick-off call.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1Before we sign, let us confirm the term sheet.Antes de assinar, vamos confirmar o resumo dos termos.
2We agreed on the twelve-month term, didn't we?A gente concordou com o prazo de doze meses, né?
3You will keep the uptime guarantee, won't you?Vocês vão manter a garantia de uptime, não vão?
4Legal can formalize it and prepare it for signature.O jurídico pode formalizar e preparar para assinatura.
5Once we have the sign-off, we proceed to the kick-off.Assim que tivermos a aprovação, seguimos para a reunião inicial.

IN CLASS -- Select Lesson

01
Who Is Rafael? -- Diagnostic + Self-Introduction
Present simple + Professional vocabulary -- 27 slides
02
Running the Meeting -- Leading Status Updates
Present continuous + Meeting vocabulary -- 29 slides
03
Presenting Numbers -- Data and KPIs for the Board
Comparatives + Numbers/Percentages -- 32 slides
04
Managing Vendors -- Negotiation Basics
Reading a vendor negotiation + would -- 28 slides
05
The Sprint Review -- Technical Updates for Non-Tech People
Reading a sprint update + relative clauses -- 28 slides
06
Handling Pushback -- Disagreeing Professionally
Reading a pushback case + first conditional -- 29 slides
07
The Board Presentation -- Executive Communication
Reading a board brief + formal connectors -- 28 slides
08
Remote Team Management -- Async Communication
Imperatives + Polite Requests -- 28 slides
09
Risk Assessment -- Reporting Problems and Solutions
Modals: should, must, might -- 28 slides
10
The International Call -- Cross-Cultural Communication
Indirect questions and clarifying language -- 28 slides
11
Project Proposal -- Pitching Ideas to Leadership
Future forms: will and be going to -- 28 slides
12
Quarterly Review -- Summarizing Results
Reading a quarterly report + present perfect -- 28 slides
13
Onboarding a New Vendor -- Contract Discussion
Second conditional + legal vocabulary -- 28 slides
14
Crisis Communication -- When Things Go Wrong
Past continuous + crisis vocabulary -- 28 slides
15
The Job Interview -- Telling Your Story
Present perfect + past simple -- 28 slides
16
The Full Interview & Program Review
Mock interview + STAR review -- 28 slides
17
Leading the Handover & Documenting the Process
Passive voice + process docs -- 28 slides
18
Difficult Conversations & Giving Feedback
Reported speech + feedback -- 28 slides
19
Negotiating & Reaching Agreement
Conditional conjunctions (unless / as long as / provided that) -- 28 slides
20
Closing the Deal & Confirming the Final Terms
Question tags (..., didn't we? / ..., won't you?) -- 28 slides

Materiais Complementares -- Aula 1

Lesson 1 — Who Is Rafael Pelizaro?

TED Talk
How Great Leaders Inspire Action -- Simon Sinek

Watch how Simon Sinek uses simple present to describe what leaders do. Listen for: "People don't buy what you do, they buy why you do it." Great model for professional introductions.

Tip: Watch with English subtitles. Note how he structures "I do X" and "We believe Y."

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
Masters of Scale — Airbnb's Brian Chesky in Handcrafted

Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky explains how he handcrafted the guest experience himself, meeting hosts door-to-door and designing an 'eleven-star experience' before scaling. He describes his role, his team, and the projects they built — ideal for hearing 'I manage...', 'we built...', 'our team...'.

Jot down every phrase Chesky uses to describe what he and his team did, and repeat them aloud to practice talking about your own role.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
How to Talk About Your Job in English

Practice phrases like "I am responsible for...", "I manage...", "My team..." Directly connected to the vocabulary of this lesson.

Tip: Watch with English subtitles. Try to describe YOUR job using the same phrases.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — Running the Meeting -- Leading Status Updates

TED Talk
Steven Rogelberg -- "The Power of You to Truly Make Meetings Work" (TEDxUNCCharlotte)

Watch how he uses present continuous when discussing what teams are doing wrong in meetings. Listen for: "People are wasting time in meetings every day." Great model for meeting vocabulary.

Tip: Watch with English subtitles. Note every time the speaker uses "is/are + verb-ing" to describe current problems.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Espresso English — Business English: Phrases for Meetings

A free sample lesson teaching 82 practical phrases for meetings: opening, giving your opinion, agreeing and disagreeing, and wrapping up.

Pick five phrases and use them in your next meeting.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
How to Make Virtual Meetings Feel More Real | Christine vs. Work

Tips for leading remote meetings effectively. Phrases like "Let me walk you through the agenda" and "Can we follow up on this?" connect directly to this lesson's vocabulary.

Tip: Watch with English subtitles. Try to lead YOUR next meeting using the same phrases.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — Presenting Numbers -- Data and KPIs for the Board

TED Talk
Hans Rosling -- "The Best Stats You've Ever Seen"

Watch how Rosling presents data using comparatives: "This country is growing faster than...", "Infant mortality is lower than..." A masterclass in presenting numbers with clarity and impact.

Tip: Watch with English subtitles. Note every comparative he uses (higher, lower, faster, better).

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Mastering the Art of Persuasion

Wharton's Jonah Berger on getting an audience to truly listen and come to your side: lower their resistance and ask questions instead of pushing facts and data at them.

Note comparison phrases like "compared to", "higher than", "more likely to".

Open episode ↗

YouTube
How to Give a Great Data Presentation

Practical tips for presenting data in meetings. Focus on the comparisons: "This metric is better than...", "Revenue grew faster than..." Directly connected to comparatives from this lesson.

Tip: Watch with English subtitles. Practice describing YOUR team's KPIs using the same patterns.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Managing Vendors -- Negotiation Basics

Series
Shark Tank (ABC) -- any episode of a pitch and negotiation

Founders pitch a proposal and then negotiate terms live with the investors. Listen for how each side asks for a discount, makes a counteroffer, and trades one term for another to reach an agreement.

Watch one negotiation with English subtitles. Write down three offers, and rewrite each one politely with would ("Would you take 10% for a longer commitment?").

Podcast
Down to Business English — The Netflix–Warner Bros. Acquisition (Part 1) | 400

The hosts explain how Netflix won a bidding battle to acquire major Warner Bros. Discovery assets — a real-world look at competitive deal-making.

Notice the language of offers and counteroffers as the hosts describe the bidding war.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Polite English: would for offers and requests"

A focused lesson on using would to sound polite and soft: "Would you consider...?", "We would accept...". Directly reinforces today's grammar on making proposals and counteroffers.

After watching, write six would sentences for a supplier negotiation, mixing a request, an offer, and a counteroffer. Read them aloud.

Lesson 5 — The Sprint Review -- Technical Updates for Non-Tech People

Series
Silicon Valley (HBO) -- Season 1, Episode 5: "Signaling Risk"

Engineers struggle to explain their product to investors and non-technical partners. Listen for the moments when a clear, simple explanation wins, and when jargon loses the room.

Watch with English subtitles. Note one technical idea a character explains, then rewrite it in one plain sentence using a relative clause ("a feature that...").

Podcast
All Ears English — How to Clarify Numbers in Workplace English

A Business English from All Ears English episode on explaining and clarifying information at work: how to confirm details, restate figures simply, and make sure a colleague really understands you.

Practice restating one technical detail in a single simple sentence, the way the hosts model it.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Relative clauses: who, which and that"

A clear lesson on defining relative clauses, exactly the grammar from today's discovery. It shows when to use who for people and that or which for things.

After watching, write six definitions of IT words (sprint, demo, backlog, bug...) using who / which / that. Read them aloud in plain English.

Lesson 6 -- Handling Pushback

Lesson 6 — Handling Pushback -- Disagreeing Professionally

Series
The Office (US) -- Season 3, Episode 23: "Beach Games"

Managers and team members disagree about decisions, push back on plans, and try to reach a compromise. Listen for how characters raise concerns and object without becoming aggressive.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two moments of pushback. Then rewrite one rude line to make it professional, using "I see your point, but...".

Podcast
BBC 6 Minute English — Is It OK to Disagree?

Neil and Beth discuss whether society is becoming too divided and teach vocabulary for handling disagreement, useful language for raising a concern and disagreeing politely at work.

Note the softening phrases used to disagree without sounding rude.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "The First Conditional"

A focused lesson on the first conditional (If + present, ... will / may + verb). Directly reinforces today's grammar discovery for explaining consequences.

After watching, write six first-conditional sentences about your project, for example "If the vendor is late, we will need a backup plan." Read them aloud.

Lesson 7 — The Board Presentation -- Executive Communication

Series
Mad Men -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Smoke Gets in Your Eyes"

Watch the final pitch scene, where Don Draper gives a short, confident presentation to a board of clients. Notice the clear overview, the single key point, and the calm, formal tone.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down the one recommendation he leaves the clients with, then rewrite it as a board update for your own project.

Podcast
Think Fast, Talk Smart — Crafting Narratives That Motivate: What's Your Strategy Story?

Stanford's Matt Abrahams and BCG's Martin Reeves show how to present a strategy as a clear story that moves an audience from today's situation to a future vision, ideal for pitching ideas to senior leaders.

Structure your next update as present situation → what's changing → one recommendation.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Linking words and connectors"

A focused lesson on connectors for addition, contrast, result and summary (in addition, however, as a result, to summarize). Directly reinforces today's grammar discovery.

After watching, write six sentences about your project, each with a different connector, for example "Costs are low. In addition, quality is high." Read them aloud in a calm, formal tone.

Lesson 8 — Remote Team Management

Series
The Office (US) -- Season 4, Episode 1: "Fun Run"

Watch how the team shares updates and asks each other for help. Notice when a request sounds direct and a bit rude, and when it sounds polite and collaborative.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two direct requests and rewrite them as polite async messages using "Could you ..." or "Would you mind ...-ing".

Disponível nos streamings: Max e Prime Video

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 386: Can You Walk Me Through This? How to Ask for Extra Help at Work

A clear episode on softening requests at work: how to politely ask a colleague to walk you through something or give extra help, using phrases that sound professional and friendly across a team.

Record a 90-second request asking a teammate for help, using 'could you' and 'would you mind'.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
How to be polite in English: Ask BBC Learning English

A focused lesson on the difference between imperatives and polite requests, including the -ing form after "would you mind". Directly reinforces today's grammar discovery.

After watching, write six short async messages about your project, mixing direct steps ("Please deploy ...") and polite requests ("Could you ...", "Would you mind ...-ing"). Read them aloud.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Risk Assessment

Series
The Martian (film) -- the "work the problem" scenes

Watch how the NASA team reports risks and proposes solutions under pressure. Notice how they name the problem, weigh likelihood and impact, and decide what they must do first.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three sentences using might, should, and must to describe one risk and one solution from the film.

Disponível no streaming: Disney+

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — The Case for Embracing Uncertainty

Nathan and Susannah Furr on the language of risk and uncertainty at work: how to reframe problems, weigh what is likely, and take action despite the unknown.

Collect the words used for likelihood and recommendation ("probably", "we should", "we must").

Open episode ↗

YouTube
'May', 'might' and 'could' - 6 Minute Grammar

A focused lesson on the difference between possibility, recommendation, and necessity, and why we use the base verb after each modal. Directly reinforces today's grammar discovery.

After watching, write six short sentences about a real risk in your project, mixing might (possibility), should (recommendation), and must (necessity). Read them aloud.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — The International Call

Film
Lost in Translation -- the hotel and meeting scenes

Watch how people from different cultures try to understand each other. Notice the misunderstandings, the role of tone, and the moments where a simple clarification would have helped.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two moments of misunderstanding and rewrite them using an indirect question, such as "Could you tell me what you mean?"

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 328: Nevermind! How to Respond to a Coworker's Mistake

A clear episode on diplomatic, indirect language: how to address a mistake or awkward moment tactfully, soften your message, and keep a friendly tone with colleagues to avoid misunderstandings.

Record a 90-second message giving feedback indirectly, keeping your tone gentle.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Indirect questions: a more polite way to ask"

A focused lesson on how indirect questions work: the introducer, the statement word order, and why we drop do, does, and did. Directly reinforces today's grammar discovery.

After watching, turn six direct questions about your project into indirect questions. Read them aloud with a calm, polite tone.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — Project Proposal

Series
Shark Tank -- any pitch from Season 1

Watch how founders pitch an idea to leadership in just a few minutes. Notice how they state the problem, give a rationale with numbers, and ask clearly for what they want.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two sentences with "going to" (a plan) and two with "will" (a promise or prediction) that the founders use.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
Think Fast, Talk Smart — Change My Mind: Using "Pre-suasion" to Influence Others

This episode covers how to pitch an idea to colleagues and win buy-in — including the research-backed move of asking for 'advice' rather than 'opinion' to get better input and more support for your proposal.

Before your next pitch, try opening by asking a decision-maker for their advice on the plan — the episode shows why that boosts buy-in.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Will or going to? Talking about the future"

A focused lesson on the difference between "will" and "be going to": decisions made now versus plans already decided, and predictions from opinion versus predictions from evidence. Directly reinforces today's grammar.

After watching, write six sentences about your next project: three with "going to" (your plans) and three with "will" (your promises and predictions).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Quarterly Review -- Summarizing Results

Series
Industry (HBO) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Induction"

Young professionals are judged on the results they deliver. Listen for how they report numbers, hit or miss targets, and summarize a quarter to senior managers under pressure.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three sentences where a character reports a result, and rewrite each one in the present perfect ("She has exceeded her target").

Podcast
Down to Business English — Tokenmaxxing and the Corporate AI Pullback (Episode 414)

The hosts report on a real business story in clear English: how companies like Uber, Amazon, and Microsoft are tracking AI spending and shifting from measuring activity to reporting actual results and productivity.

Listen for how the hosts report status, what's on track, what's a setback, and the numbers behind it.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "The present perfect: have / has + past participle"

A focused lesson on the form and meaning of the present perfect, including so far, already and yet. Directly reinforces today's grammar discovery on reporting results.

After watching, write six present-perfect sentences about your last quarter, mixing a target, a metric, a deliverable, and one setback. Read them aloud.

Lesson 13 — Onboarding a New Vendor

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Watch how the lawyers read contracts, question clauses, and negotiate terms. Notice when they explore a hypothetical risk with "if" before they sign anything.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two clauses they discuss, and rewrite one as a second conditional ("If the client missed the deadline, they would ...").

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
The Business English Podcast — Power Phrases for Strategy and Market Talks (BEP 154)

A free episode on high-stakes business negotiation: how to buy time, hold your position and use precise power phrases when discussing strategy and terms without over-committing. The same negotiation language you need to talk through a contract.

Note 3 phrases for staying firm politely, then use them in a mock contract negotiation.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
The second conditional - 6 Minute Grammar

A focused lesson on the form and meaning of the second conditional, including "If I were you ..." for advice. Directly reinforces today's grammar discovery.

After watching, write six second-conditional sentences about a vendor contract, mixing penalty, liability, scope, and "If I were you ..." advice. Read them aloud.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Crisis Communication

Series
The IT Crowd -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Yesterday's Jam"

A comedy about an IT team dealing with systems that go wrong. Watch how they describe what was happening when things broke, and notice the gap between a calm response and a panicked one.

Watch with English subtitles. Write three past-continuous sentences about what the characters were doing when a problem started ("The server was failing while...").

Disponível no streaming: Prime Video

Podcast
Google SRE — Postmortems with Ayelet Sachto (SRE Prodcast, Ep. 9)

Google SRE Ayelet Sachto explains how strong teams learn from outages: writing a clear record of the incident, keeping postmortems blameless, and turning lessons into concrete action items. Full transcript on the official Google SRE site.

Read along with the on-page transcript to catch incident vocabulary like 'blameless', 'root cause' and 'action item'.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
The Race: Shipwrecked. Learn to use past simple and past continuous - Episode 7

A focused lesson on the form and meaning of the past continuous, including the contrast with the past simple using "when" and "while". Directly reinforces today's grammar discovery.

After watching, write six past-continuous sentences about an outage, mixing incident, severity, war room, and a "when/while" interruption. Read them aloud.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — The Job Interview

Film
"The Pursuit of Happyness" -- the interview scene

Chris Gardner tells his professional story under pressure. Watch how he turns a difficult past into a confident answer, mixing what he has done over time with one finished example.

Watch with English subtitles. Write three sentences in the present perfect about your career and two in the past simple about a finished moment.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Harvard Business Review — How to Answer the Question, "Tell Me About Yourself"

In this HBR video from the Christine vs. Work series, Christine Liu breaks down why interviewers ask this question and how to build a focused answer around what the role actually needs instead of reciting your resume.

Pause the video after each tip and draft one sentence about yourself, then say it out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "Present perfect vs past simple"

A focused lesson on when to use the present perfect (experience up to now, ever/never/for/since) and the past simple (finished time, last year/ago). Directly reinforces today's grammar.

After watching, write six sentences about your career, mixing track record, strengths, and one "last year" example. Read them aloud.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — The Full Interview & Program Review

Series
"Silicon Valley" -- the pitch and panel scenes
