Watch how the team prepares and rehearses before facing a tough panel. Notice how the strongest answers tell a clear story with a result, while weak ones are just opinions.

Watch with English subtitles. Pick one scene and rewrite an answer using the STAR method: situation, action (past simple), and a result with a number.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Harvard Business Review — Stand Out in a Job Interview (The Harvard Business Review Guide)

HBR contributing editor Amy Gallo explains how to prepare for a job interview: research the role, choose concrete stories from your experience, and articulate the result you delivered so your answers prove your value.

Listen for the story structure she recommends, then rehearse one of your own examples using the same shape.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
STAR Method Interview: How to Answer Behavioral Questions (With Examples)

Real example answers for the most common interview questions, built step by step with STAR. Directly reinforces today's structure and the present perfect vs past simple switch.

After watching, prepare three STAR stories from your career and rehearse them out loud until you can showcase each one in under two minutes.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Leading the Handover & Documenting the Process

Series
"The Office" -- onboarding and handover episodes

Watch scenes where a manager explains how the office works to a new hire. Notice how the clearest explanations focus on the process, not the person: "the form is filled out", "the order is approved".

Watch with English subtitles. Pick one scene and rewrite three steps in the passive voice: present (is documented) and past (was approved).

Disponível nos streamings: Max e Prime Video

Podcast
Dear HBR: — Onboarding Remotely (Bonus)

In this Dear HBR: episode, hosts Alison Beard and Dan McGinn and Boston University professor Siobhan O'Mahony work through how new hires can onboard well: building relationships, establishing credibility, and getting up to speed when you can't rely on in-person cues.

Note the questions they suggest a new hire should ask early on, and write down which apply to your own onboarding.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
"The Passive Voice in English -- When and How to Use It"

A clear lesson on be + past participle, present and past, and when to add by + the agent. Directly reinforces today's grammar with business examples.

After watching, take five active sentences about your work ("We test the build") and turn each one into the passive ("The build is tested"). Read them out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Difficult Conversations & Giving Feedback

Series
"Ted Lasso" -- the difficult one-on-one scenes

Watch scenes where a leader gives honest feedback while staying kind and calm. Notice how he addresses the behavior, acknowledges the person's side, and reassures them instead of attacking.

Watch with English subtitles. Pick one feedback scene and report three lines in reported speech: "He told her she...", "She said she would...".

Disponível nos streamings: Apple TV+ e Prime Video

Podcast
Dear HBR: — Hard Conversations

Hosts Alison Beard and Dan McGinn with Harvard Business School professor Leslie John talk through dreaded workplace conversations: setting a boss straight, managing a colleague who wanted your job, and confronting a negative team member without making it personal.

Listen for how they separate the behavior from the person, and prepare one opening line before your next tough talk.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Can you use REPORTED SPEECH? Grammar Lesson + Examples

A clear lesson on reporting what people said: the verb moves one step back (is to was, will to would), and the difference between say and tell. Directly reinforces today's grammar.

After watching, take five things teammates said this week ("The build is ready", "I will test it") and report each one: "He said the build was ready", "She told me she would test it".

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Negotiating & Reaching Agreement

Series
"Suits" -- the negotiation and closing scenes

Watch scenes where lawyers negotiate a deal: they use their leverage, set conditions, and bargain to a win-win. Notice how they look for common ground before they push on the terms.

Watch with English subtitles. Pick one negotiation scene and write three conditions using "unless", "as long as", and "provided that".

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — To Negotiate Better, Start with Yourself

William Ury, cofounder of Harvard's Program on Negotiation and coauthor of Getting to Yes, argues that the biggest obstacle in any negotiation is usually yourself, and shares methods for reaching a win-win agreement.

Before your next negotiation, try Ury's advice: define your best alternative and your bottom line first.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
ADVANCED CONDITIONALS unless, provided that, as long as, in case, on condition that, supposing

A clear lesson on setting conditions: "unless" means "if not", while "as long as" and "provided that" mean "only if". The verb after them stays in the present, never "will". Directly reinforces today's grammar.

After watching, write five conditions for a real deal at work: "We can renew provided that you cut the cost", "Unless the SLA improves, we will switch vendors".

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — Closing the Deal

Series
"Suits" -- the contract signing scenes

Watch scenes where the deal is finally closed: the lawyers confirm the final terms, prepare the documents for signature, and move to the formal sign-off. Notice how they double-check each detail before anyone signs.

Watch with English subtitles. Pick one signing scene and write three confirmations using question tags: "We agreed on this, didn't we?", "You will deliver on time, won't you?"

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — How CEOs Can Drive Sales — or Kill Deals

INSEAD professor Christoph Senn explains how senior leaders engage with key customers to close major B2B deals, describing five executive archetypes and how account teams can use their leader's style to get a deal over the line.

Listen for what actually moves a deal to closing, and jot down which archetype fits a leader you work with.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Grammar: Question tags - BBC English Masterclass

A clear lesson on question tags: a positive statement takes a negative tag, and a negative statement takes a positive tag, repeating the auxiliary verb. The exact tool you use to confirm the final terms of a deal. Directly reinforces today's grammar.

After watching, write five confirmations for a real deal at work: "We finalized the budget, didn't we?", "You will send the sign-off, won't you?"

Assistir no YouTube ↗

The Manager Key Words The Code In Context Practice Your Turn Wrap-Up
Lesson 1 -- Business English for IT

Who Is
Rafael Pelizaro?

B1 -- 60 minutes -- Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Manager

Let's Talk About You

"Tell me about your work as a project manager."
"What do you enjoy most about leading IT teams?"
Chapter 1: The Manager

Your English Goals

"When do you need English the most at work?"
"What would change in your career if you spoke fluent English?"
Chapter 2: Key Words

Your Professional
Vocabulary

Eight essential words for IT project management

Chapter 2: Key Words

Click on each card to reveal

To be in charge of a project or team

Manage

To be in charge of and make decisions about a project or team
"I manage three IT projects for a major bank in Brazil."
To direct and guide a team toward a goal

Lead

To direct and guide a team toward a goal
"Rafael leads a remote team of twelve developers."
To put a plan or system into action

Implement

To put a plan, system, or idea into action
"We plan to implement the new ERP module next quarter."
To release software to a production environment

Deploy

To release software or updates to a production environment
"The IT team will deploy the update on Friday."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2: Key Words

Click on each card to reveal

To give a formal account of progress

Report

To give a formal account of progress or results to someone
"I need to report our progress to the board this afternoon."
A person with interest in a project's outcome

Stakeholder

A person with an interest or concern in a project's outcome
"The stakeholders are expecting a detailed project update."
An external company that provides products or services

Vendor

An external company that provides products or services
"We are negotiating with a new vendor for cloud services."
An important stage in the development of a project

Milestone

An important stage or event in the development of a project
"We reached an important milestone last month."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 3: The Code

The Grammar
Behind the Words

Present Simple -- describing routines and responsibilities

Chapter 3: The Code

What do these sentences have in common?

Rafael manages technology systems.
He leads a remote team.
She reports progress every week.
The team deploys updates on Fridays.
SubjectVerb FormExample
I / You / We / Theybase formI manage projects.
He / She / Itverb + s/esHe manages projects.
Chapter 3: The Code

Common Mistake

"He lead a team of twelve developers."

"He leads a team of twelve developers."

Chapter 3: The Code

Fill in the Blanks

Rafael ___manages technology systems for banks.
He ___leads meetings with his remote team every day.
The company ___implements new solutions regularly.
They ___deploy updates every Friday.
She ___reports progress to the board every week.
Chapter 4: In Context

A Conversation at
a Tech Conference

Rafael meets Sarah Chen from Singapore

Chapter 4: In Context

The Conversation

SC
Sarah Chen
"Hi! I am Sarah Chen, IT Director at GlobalTech in Singapore. Are you attending the cloud infrastructure panel?"
RP
Rafael Pelizaro
"Nice to meet you, Sarah! I am Rafael Pelizaro. I manage IT projects for banks and e-commerce companies here in Brazil."
SC
Sarah Chen
"That sounds interesting! What kind of projects do you lead?"
RP
Rafael Pelizaro
"I lead a remote team of twelve developers. We implement and deploy technology systems. Our latest project was a payment platform for a major bank."
SC
Sarah Chen
"Impressive! Do you work with international vendors?"
RP
Rafael Pelizaro
"Yes, we work with several vendors for cloud services. I report our progress to stakeholders every week."
SC
Sarah Chen
"That is great experience! We are looking for partners in Latin America. Could we connect after the panel?"
RP
Rafael Pelizaro
"Absolutely! I would love to discuss this further. Here is my business card."
Chapter 4: In Context

Did You Understand?

1. Where is Sarah Chen from?
She is from Singapore. She is an IT Director at GlobalTech.
2. What does Sarah's company want in Latin America?
They are looking for partners in Latin America.
3. How often does Rafael report to stakeholders?
He reports to stakeholders every week.
Chapter 4: In Context

Listening Challenge

Listen to Rafael introducing himself. Answer the questions.

0:000:00
1. Where is Rafael based?
He is based in São Paulo, Brazil.
2. What milestone did his team reach last quarter?
They deployed a new payment platform.
Chapter 4: In Context

Listening Part 2

Listen to a follow-up question after Rafael's presentation.

0:000:00
1. What did the person ask about?
The deployment process, the number of cloud vendors, and how Rafael manages communication across time zones.
Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Challenge

Six professional situations -- respond in English

Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
A colleague at a conference asks: "What do you do?" Introduce yourself professionally.
"I am an IT Project Manager. I manage technology systems for banks and e-commerce companies."
Chapter 5: Practice

Spot the Error

"I manage three project for a bank."
"I manage three projects for a bank."
"He lead a team of twelve developers."
"He leads a team of twelve developers."
"We deploy the update in Friday."
"We deploy the update on Friday."
"I report to the stakeholder every weeks."
"I report to the stakeholders every week."
"She implement the new system last month."
"She implemented the new system last month."
0 / 5 errors found
Chapter 5: Practice

Oral Drilling

1. A recruiter asks your name and role. What do you say?
"I am Rafael Pelizaro. I am an IT Project Manager based in São Paulo."
2. Someone asks what you do every day. Respond.
"I manage IT projects, lead meetings, and report progress to stakeholders."
3. A colleague asks about your team. Answer.
"I lead a remote team of twelve developers."
4. Someone asks about your latest project. What do you say?
"We deployed a new payment platform for a major bank last quarter."
5. A vendor asks how you work together. Respond.
"We work with several vendors for cloud services and infrastructure."
6. You want to connect with someone at a conference. What do you say?
"I would love to discuss this further. Could we connect after the session?"
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Time to Speak

From guided to free -- show what you learned

Chapter 6: Your Turn

Role-Play: Tech Conference

You are at a tech conference in São Paulo. Sarah Chen from Singapore asks about your work. Introduce yourself, describe your role, and mention a recent project.

manage lead deploy stakeholder milestone
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Role-Play: Post-Presentation Q&A

You just finished a presentation about your latest deployment. A recruiter from a global company asks about your experience. Answer confidently.

implement vendor report
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Role-Play: Conference Dinner

You are at the conference dinner. A colleague from Berlin asks about your career goals. Have a natural conversation about your plans, challenges, and ambitions.

No keywords. Speak freely.

Chapter 6: Your Turn

Error Correction

Errors from today's role-plays:

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

Record Your Self-Introduction

Deliver a 90-second professional self-introduction to your teacher. Use the vocabulary from today's lesson.
Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

What's Next

Homework
Record a 90-second professional self-introduction. Use at least 5 vocabulary words from today.
Next Lesson
Running the Meeting -- Leading Status Updates. Complete the Pre-class before our next session.
What I Learned

Lesson 1 Checklist

I can introduce myself professionally in English.
I know 8 IT project management vocabulary words.
I can use Present Simple to describe my job and routines.
I can describe my team and responsibilities.
I can talk about a recent project or milestone.
I can understand a professional self-introduction in English.
I can have a professional conversation at a conference.
I feel more confident about networking in English.
Lesson 2 -- Business English for IT

Running the
Meeting

B1 -- 60 minutes -- Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Check-In

Quick Callback

Last class, you introduced yourself at a conference.

Quick recap: What do you manage? Who do you lead?

Chapter 1: The Check-In

Your Meetings

Think about your weekly meetings at work:

  • How often do you have meetings?
  • What do you usually discuss?
  • What is the hardest part about leading meetings?
Chapter 2: Meeting Tools

Meeting
Tools

8 essential words for every meeting

Chapter 2: Meeting Tools

Click on each card to reveal

A list of topics to be discussed during a meeting.

Agenda

A list of topics to be discussed during a meeting.

"Let me share the agenda for today's meeting."

A specific task assigned to someone during a meeting.

Action item

A specific task assigned to someone during or after a meeting.

"We have three action items from last week."

To check on or continue something that was discussed.

Follow up

To check on or continue something that was discussed.

"I will follow up with the vendor about the delivery."

A report on the current progress of a project or task.

Status update

A report on the current progress of a project or task.

"Can you give us a status update on the deployment?"

0 / 4 words revealed

Chapter 2: Meeting Tools

4 more words

The final date by which something must be completed.

Deadline

The final date by which something must be completed.

"The deadline for the new module is next Friday."

To decide the order of importance of tasks.

Prioritize

To decide the order of importance of tasks.

"We need to prioritize the critical bugs."

To change the planned time or date of a meeting.

Reschedule

To change the planned time or date of a meeting.

"Can we reschedule the meeting to Thursday?"

The official written record of what was discussed.

Minutes

The official written record of what was discussed in a meeting.

"I will send the meeting minutes by end of day."

0 / 4 words revealed

Chapter 2: Meeting Tools

The Agenda

Weekly Status Meeting -- Q2 Sprint Review
Monday, 10:00 AM (Zoom)
Attendees: Rafael Pelizaro (PM), Lisa Park (QA), Dev Team
1. Deployment status update
2. QA testing report
3. Vendor follow-up
4. Action items review
5. Next week's deadlines
Chapter 3: The Pattern

The
Pattern

Present Continuous -- discover the rule

Chapter 3: The Pattern

What do these sentences have in common?

Rafael is leading the status meeting right now.

Lisa is preparing the QA report.

They are deploying the update this afternoon.

We are rescheduling the demo for Friday.

SubjectFormExample
Iam + verb-ingI am preparing the agenda.
He/She/Itis + verb-ingHe is leading the meeting.
We/You/Theyare + verb-ingWe are discussing the deadlines.
Chapter 3: The Pattern

Common Mistake

✗

"He leading the meeting."

✓

"He is leading the meeting."

Chapter 3: The Pattern

Complete with Present Continuous

"I ___ (prepare) the agenda right now."

am preparing

"Lisa ___ (test) the payment module."

is testing

"We ___ (reschedule) the demo for Friday."

are rescheduling

"They ___ (deploy) the update this afternoon."

are deploying

"He ___ (follow up) with the vendor."

is following up

Chapter 4: The Status Call

The Status
Call

Monday morning -- Zoom meeting

Chapter 4: The Status Call

Monday Status Meeting

RP Rafael

"Good morning, everyone. Let me share the agenda for today's meeting. We have three items: the deployment status, the QA report, and next week's deadlines."

LP Lisa Park

"Good morning, Rafael. Before we start, I have a quick status update on the QA testing."

RP Rafael

"Sure, go ahead, Lisa. What is the current status?"

LP Lisa Park

"We are testing the payment module right now. My team is finding some critical bugs. I am prioritizing them, but we might need to reschedule the release."

RP Rafael

"I see. How many critical bugs are you finding?"

LP Lisa Park

"Three so far. We are working on fixes, but the deadline is tight. Can we follow up on this tomorrow?"

RP Rafael

"Absolutely. I will add that as an action item. Let me take the minutes and send them to the team."

LP Lisa Park

"That sounds great. I will prepare a detailed report for tomorrow's follow-up."

Chapter 4: The Status Call

Did you understand?

What is Lisa's team finding in the payment module?

They are finding critical bugs.

What does Lisa want to do about the release?

She wants to reschedule it because the deadline is tight.

What will Rafael do after the meeting?

He will take the minutes and send them to the team.

Chapter 4: The Status Call

Listening 1: Status Meeting

Listen to the full status meeting excerpt.

0:00 / 0:00

What is the team deploying on Friday?

The new payment module.

What is Rafael following up on with the vendor?

The cloud service agreement.

Chapter 4: The Status Call

Listening 2: Follow-Up Call

Lisa is calling about the Friday deployment.

0:00 / 0:00

Why is Lisa calling?

She wants to reschedule the deployment to next Tuesday.

What will Lisa send by end of day?

The updated status report with all the action items.

Chapter 4: The Status Call

Oral Drilling

1. Your team asks what you are working on today. Respond.

"I am preparing the agenda for our status meeting and following up on last week's action items."

2. A colleague asks when the meeting is. Answer.

"We are meeting at 10 AM on Monday via Zoom."

3. Someone asks about the QA status. What do you say?

"The QA team is testing the payment module right now."

4. Your manager asks about the vendor. Respond.

"I am following up with the vendor about the cloud service agreement."

5. A team member asks about the deadline. Answer.

"The deadline for the deployment is next Friday."

6. Someone asks what happens after the meeting. Respond.

"I will send the minutes and the action items to everyone."

Chapter 5: Your Meeting

Your
Meeting

Practice with real situations

Chapter 5: Your Meeting

Quick Challenge

0 / 6

Question 1 of 6

Your meeting starts in 5 minutes. You need to share the topics. What do you say?

"Let me share the agenda for today's meeting."

Chapter 5: Your Meeting

Spot the Error

0 / 5 errors found

"We discussing the new budget."

We are discussing the new budget.

"She is prepare the QA report."

She is preparing the QA report.

"I will follow up in the action items."

I will follow up on the action items.

"The deadline is on the next Friday."

The deadline is next Friday. (no "on the")

"He is prioritize the critical bugs."

He is prioritizing the critical bugs.

Chapter 5: Your Meeting

More Practice

Use Present Continuous to describe what is happening in YOUR work right now:

  • What project are you working on this week?
  • What is your team doing right now?
  • Are you following up on anything from last week?
  • Is anyone on your team approaching a deadline?
Chapter 6: Lead the Room

Lead the
Room

From guided to free -- show what you learned

Guided Practice

Role-Play: Opening a Status Meeting

You are opening your Monday status meeting. Greet the team, share the agenda, and ask for updates on current projects.

agenda status update action item deadline follow up
Semi-Free Practice

Role-Play: Handling a Delay

A team member reports a delay on the deployment. Ask questions, suggest solutions, and decide whether to reschedule.

reschedule prioritize deadline
Chapter 6: Lead the Room

Role-Play: Full Status Meeting

Run a complete 5-minute status meeting. Your teacher will play different team members. Open the meeting, manage updates, handle problems, assign action items, and close.

Use everything you learned today. No keywords, no script.

Chapter 6: Lead the Room

Error Correction

Delayed feedback from the role-plays:

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

Your Status Update

Give a 30-second status update to your teacher. Use:

  • Present Continuous (am/is/are + verb-ing)
  • At least 4 vocabulary words from today
Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

What's Next

Lesson 2 is almost complete!

Your teacher will share your homework for next class.

Remember to complete your Pre-class exercises before the next lesson.

What I Learned

Lesson 2 Checklist

I can use 8 essential meeting vocabulary words.
I can use Present Continuous for current actions and future arrangements.
I can open a meeting and share an agenda.
I can give and receive status updates.
I can handle meeting problems like delays and rescheduling.
I can assign action items and take minutes.
I can understand a meeting dialogue and follow-up call.
I feel more confident leading meetings in English.
